title: EXPOSÉ I GENERALITIES ON FINITENESS CONDITIONS IN DERIVED CATEGORIES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ I GENERALITIES ON FINITENESS CONDITIONS IN DERIVED CATEGORIES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ I
GENERALITIES ON FINITENESS CONDITIONS IN DERIVED CATEGORIESby L. Illusie
0. Introduction
The purpose of Exposés I to IV is to develop, with the generality appropriate to this Seminar, the formalism of finiteness conditions in the derived categories of ringed topoi.

As was said in Exp. 0, it is the need to define, on arbitrary schemes, "Grothendieck groups" possessing good variance properties that made it necessary to generalize and render more flexible the finiteness notions used up to now.

A classical notion such as that of a coherent sheaf on a ringed space \((X,\underline{\mathcal O}_X)\) becomes uninteresting as soon as \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\) is no longer coherent. One might think of replacing it by the notion of finite presentation, but the latter has the drawback that the kernel of an epimorphism of Modules of finite presentation is in general no longer of finite presentation. One arrives at a satisfactory notion by remarking that, if \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\) is coherent, a coherent sheaf \(F\) is not only of finite presentation but of finite \(n\)-presentation for every \(n\in\mathbb N\), "finite \(n\)-presentation" meaning that there exists locally an exact sequence
\[L_n\longrightarrow L_{n-1}\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow L_0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0\;,\]

where the \(L_i\) are free of finite type. The sheaf \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\) being no longer assumed coherent, let us say that an \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\)-Module \(F\) is pseudo-coherent if it satisfies the preceding condition, i.e. is of finite \(n\)-presentation for every \(n\). Then the notion of pseudo-coherence possesses, with respect to short exact sequences, the same stability property as the notion of coherence: if two of the terms of a short exact sequence of \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\)-Modules are pseudo-coherent, so is the third. One easily generalizes the preceding notion to complexes of sheaves. A complex \(F\) of \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\)-Modules is said to be pseudo-coherent if it is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent for every \(n\in\mathbb Z\), "\(n\)-pseudo-coherent" meaning that there exists locally a quasi-isomorphism \(L\longrightarrow F\), where \(L\) is a complex with degrees bounded above and with components free of finite type in degrees \(\geq n\). For a complex \(F\) reduced to degree 0, it amounts to the same to say that \(F\) is pseudo-coherent as a complex or as a Module. The notion of pseudo-coherent complex has excellent stability virtues, described in Exp. I §§ 1 and 2. First of all, it is a notion stable under isomorphism in the derived category \(D(X)\) of the category of \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\)-Modules; better still, if two of the objects of a distinguished triangle of \(D(X)\) are pseudo-coherent, so is the third: in other words, the full subcategory \(D(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(D(X)\) formed by the pseudo-coherent complexes is a triangulated subcategory. Moreover, pseudo-coherence is preserved under derived inverse image and derived tensor product. In the case where \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\) is coherent, to say that a complex \(F\) is pseudo-coherent simply means that \(F\) has cohomology locally bounded above and that all the \(\underline H^i(F)\) are coherent.

If \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\) is a sheaf of regular local rings, every coherent \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\)-Module \(F\) admits locally a finite left resolution by Modules free of finite type, i.e. there exists locally an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow L_n\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow L_0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0\;,\]

where the \(L_i\) are free of finite type. It is therefore natural, when \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\) is an arbitrary sheaf of local rings, to take an interest in those Modules which enjoy the preceding property. Such Modules are called perfect. More generally, one says that a complex \(F\) of \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\)-Modules is perfect if there exists locally a quasi-isomorphism \(L\longrightarrow F\), where \(L\) is a complex with bounded degrees and with components free of finite type. One shows in Exp. I § 3 that the full subcategory \(D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) of \(D(X)\) formed by the perfect complexes is, like \(D(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), a triangulated subcategory, "stable" under derived inverse image and derived tensor product. We evidently have \(D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}}\subset D(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), but the inclusion is strict in general. Pseudo-coherence can be redefined "by passage to the limit" from perfection: a complex \(F\) is pseudo-coherent if and only if, for every \(n\in\mathbb Z\), \(F\) can be "locally approximated to order \(n\) by a perfect complex", by which one means that there exists locally a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &R\ar[dl]&\\ L\ar[rr]&&F\ar[ul] }\;,\]

where \(L\) is perfect and \(R\) is acyclic in degrees \(\geq n\). Conversely, perfection is recovered from pseudo-coherence by means of an additional regularity condition: a complex is perfect if and only if it is pseudo-coherent and locally of finite tor-dimension (Exp. I § 5). One thus recovers the fact that, if the local rings are regular, every coherent sheaf is perfect, and more generally every pseudo-coherent complex with locally bounded cohomology is perfect.

Pseudo-coherence and perfection are the two fundamental finiteness notions with which we shall work in this Seminar. §§ 1 to 5 of Exposé I are devoted to their definition and to the establishment of their elementary stability properties. For this, one uses only two or three local properties of the category of Modules free of finite type with respect to the category of all Modules. It thus proves convenient – and useful, notably in Exp. II – to axiomatize the situation, by introducing a notion of pseudo-coherence (resp. perfection) in a category fibered over a site, relative to a fibered subcategory satisfying suitable conditions.
§§ 6 to 8 of Exp. I generalize to perfect complexes a certain number of notions well known for locally free sheaves of finite type: rank, duality, trace of an endomorphism.

In Exp. II, one examines the problem of "the existence of global resolutions": under what conditions, on a ringed topos \((X,\underline{\mathcal O}_X)\), is a pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) complex globally isomorphic in \(D(X)\) to a complex of locally free Modules of finite type? The importance of this question resides essentially in the fact that one does not know, for the moment, how to generalize to perfect complexes certain usual constructions on locally free Modules: exterior and symmetric powers, Chern classes (for more details on this subject, see Exp. XIV). This is why it is convenient to have at one's disposal manageable sufficient criteria for the existence of global resolutions, which makes it possible to reduce certain questions about pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) complexes to analogous questions about locally free sheaves of finite type. Such criteria are given in Exp. II. One shows in particular that global resolutions exist in the following cases: \(X\) is the Zariski topos of an affine scheme, or more generally of a quasi-compact scheme possessing an ample invertible sheaf, or even an ample family of invertible sheaves in the sense of Kleiman (for example a regular scheme); or again \(X\) is the topos of sheaves of sets on a compact topological space, \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\) the sheaf of continuous complex-valued functions. By way of illustration – and to bring out the flexibility of the notions introduced – one gives in an appendix a "purely sheaf-theoretic" definition of the index of a family of elliptic operators.

Exp. III studies the stability of the finiteness conditions under derived direct image. To obtain reasonable statements, it is appropriate to "relativize" the notions of pseudo-coherence and perfection. One places oneself here in the framework of ordinary schemes, which suffices for the Seminar, but there is no doubt that sooner or later an analogous theory will have to be developed for relative schemes or analytic spaces. Let \(p:X\to S\) be an \(S\)-scheme locally of finite type and let \(F\) be a complex of \(\underline{\mathcal O}_X\)-Modules; one says that \(F\) is pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) relative to \(p\) if one can locally embed (by a closed immersion) \(X\) in a smooth \(S\)-scheme \(X'\) in such a way that \(F\), extended by zero over \(X'\), is pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect). The notion of pseudo-coherence relative to \(p\) is interesting above all in the case where \(S\) is not locally noetherian, for otherwise it coincides with the notion of ordinary pseudo-coherence. On the other hand, as one may expect, to say that \(F\) is perfect rel. to \(p\) is equivalent to saying that \(F\) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(p\) and locally of finite tor-dimension rel. to \(p\) (i.e. rel. to the sheaf of rings \(p^{-1}(\underline{\mathcal O}_S)\)). The central theorem of Exp. III is the finiteness theorem, which asserts (in a somewhat more precise form) that, if \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) is a proper morphism of \(S\)-schemes locally of finite type, the functor \(\mathbf Rf_*\) takes complexes pseudo-coherent rel. to \(S\) to complexes pseudo-coherent rel. to \(S\); this theorem is in reality a conjecture, but is nevertheless proved in the following two particular cases: a) \(S\) is locally noetherian b) \(f\) is projective; unfortunately, it seems that the extension to the general case is of the same order of difficulty as the analogous theorem in Analytic Geometry (Grauert's "theorem"). Combining the finiteness theorem with an essentially trivial formula called the projection formula, one obtains manageable criteria for the stability of relative perfection under direct image. One recovers, as a corollary, "Grauert's continuity and semi-continuity theorems" (EGA III 7.6).

Exp. IV translates the results of Exp. I to III into the language of "Grothendieck groups". On a ringed topos \((X,\underline{\mathcal O}_X)\), one denotes by \(K^{\bullet}(X)\) (resp. \(K_{\bullet}(X)\)) the "Grothendieck group" of the category of complexes which are perfect and of finite tor-dimension (resp. pseudo-coherent and with bounded cohomology). The group \(K^{\bullet}(X)\) is a ring, and \(K_{\bullet}(X)\) a module over \(K^{\bullet}(X)\). \(K^{\bullet}\) is a contravariant functor (as the dot at the top suggests) while \(K_{\bullet}\) is a covariant functor for proper and pseudo-coherent morphisms (of schemes or analytic spaces, and provided the finiteness theorem is available...). There are also unusual variances, covariance of \(K^{\bullet}\), contravariance of \(K_{\bullet}\), for morphisms satisfying suitable regularity hypotheses. Finally, the globalization criteria of Exp. II make it possible to establish the link with the "Grothendieck groups" defined naively from coherent sheaves or from locally free sheaves of finite type.
1. Preliminary definitions
1.1. Fibered categories with additive (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated) fibres

1.1.1. Let \(\underline S\) be a category, and let \(\underline C\) be a fibered \(\underline S\)-category (SGA 1 VI 6.1) whose fibres are additive (resp. triangulated) categories. We shall say that \(\underline C\) is an additive \(\underline S\)-category (resp. triangulated) (or that \(\underline C\) is additive (resp. triangulated) over \(\underline S\)) if, for every arrow \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) of \(\underline S\), the inverse image functor (determined up to unique isomorphism) \(f^*:\underline C_Y\longrightarrow\underline C_X\) is additive (resp. exact). We shall say that \(\underline C\) is an abelian \(\underline S\)-category if \(\underline C\) is an additive \(\underline S\)-category with abelian fibres, and that \(\underline C\) is a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category if in addition the inverse image functors are exact.

1.1.2. Let \(\underline C\) be a triangulated \(\underline S\)-category. One defines, for example with the aid of a cleavage of \(\underline C\) (SGA 1 VI 7.1), a fibered \(\underline S\)-category \(\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C)\) whose fibre at each \(X\in\operatorname{ob}(\underline S)\) is the category \(\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C_X)\) of distinguished triangles of \(\underline C_X\).

1.1.3. Let \(\underline C\) and \(\underline C'\) be additive (resp. triangulated) \(\underline S\)-categories and \(F:\underline C\to\underline C'\) a functor. One says that \(F\) is an additive \(\underline S\)-functor (resp. exact) if \(F\) is a cartesian \(\underline S\)-functor which is additive (resp. exact) on each fibre. If \(\underline C\) and \(\underline C'\) are triangulated \(\underline S\)-categories and if \(F\) is an exact \(\underline S\)-functor, then \(F\) defines a cartesian \(\underline S\)-functor \(\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C')\).

1.1.4. Let \(\underline C\) be a triangulated \(\underline S\)-category, and let \(\underline C'\) be a full fibered subcategory of \(\underline C\). One says that \(\underline C'\) is a triangulated sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) if, for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(\underline C'_X\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(\underline C_X\) ([V] chap. I § 1 2.3).

1.1.5. Let \(\underline C\) be an additive \(\underline S\)-category. One defines (SGA 4 XVII 2.2, 2.4) an additive (resp. triangulated) \(\underline S\)-category \(C(\underline C)\), (resp. \(K(\underline C)\)), whose fibre at \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) is the category \(C(\underline C_X)\) (resp. \(K(\underline C_X)\)) of complexes (1) (resp. complexes "up to homotopy") of \(\underline C_X\). If \(\underline C\) is a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category, one defines (loc. cit.) a triangulated \(\underline S\)-category \(D(\underline C)\), whose fibre at \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) is the derived category \(D(\underline C_X)\); if \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) is an arrow of \(\underline S\), we shall denote by \(f^*:D(\underline C_Y)\longrightarrow D(\underline C_X)\) the inverse image functor, which is deduced from \(f^*:K(\underline C_Y)\longrightarrow K(\underline C_X)\) by simple passage to the quotient (2). One defines full triangulated sub-\(\underline S\)-categories \(K^*(\underline C)\) (resp. \(D^*(\underline C)\)) (\(*=+,-,b\)) by the usual degree conditions.
1.2. Terminology.
Let \(\underline S\) be a site, \(\underline C\) a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category (1.1), \(\underline C_0\) a strictly full fibered sub-\(\underline S\)-category.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_X\), for \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\). The set of the \(f:U\longrightarrow X\) of \(\underline S_{/X}\) such that there exists an epimorphism \(E\longrightarrow f^*F\) with \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0U}\) is a sieve of \(X\). If this sieve is covering, we shall say that \(F\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type (or simply of finite type if there is no fear of confusion).

We shall say that \(\underline C_0\) is locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms if the following condition is satisfied: for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) and every epimorphism \(u:E\to F\) of \(\underline C_X\) with \(E\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\), the kernel of \(u\) is locally in \(\underline C_0\) (i.e. the set of the \(f:U\longrightarrow X\) of \(\underline S_{/X}\) such that \(f^*\operatorname{Ker}(u)\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0U}\) is a refinement of \(X\)).

By the standard procedure of decomposing a long exact sequence into short exact sequences, one sees that the preceding condition is equivalent to the following (apparently stronger) condition: for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) and every exact sequence of \(\underline C_X\)
\[(*)\qquad 0\longrightarrow E^0\longrightarrow E^1\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E^n\longrightarrow0\]
with \(E^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\) for \(1\leq i\leq n\), \(E^0\) is locally in \(\underline C_0\).
We shall say that \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms if, for every exact sequence as above such that \((*)\), \(E^0\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type.

On the other hand, we shall say that \(\underline C_0\) is locally liftable in \(\underline C\) if, for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), every epimorphism \(u:E\to F\) of \(\underline C_X\) with \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\) admits locally a section. For \(\underline C_0\) to be locally liftable in \(\underline C\), it is necessary and sufficient that, for every diagram of \(\underline C_X\) (\(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\))
\[\xymatrix{ &H\ar[d]\\ E\ar@{->>}[r]^u&F }\]
where \(u\) is an epimorphism and \(H\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\), the set of the \(U\) above \(X\) such that there exists a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ &H_U\ar[d]\ar[dl]\\ E_U\ar@{->>}[r]_{u_U}&F_U }\]
is a refinement of \(X\).

We shall say that \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\) if the following weaker condition is satisfied: for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and every epimorphism \(u:E\to F\) of \(\underline C_X\) with \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\), the set of the \(f:U\longrightarrow X\) of \(\underline S_{/X}\) such that there exists \(v:F'\longrightarrow f^*E\) with \(F'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0U}\) such that \(f^*(u)v\) is an epimorphism, is a refinement of \(X\).
This condition is equivalent to the following: for every diagram of \(\underline C_X\)
\[\xymatrix{ &H\ar[d]\\ E\ar@{->>}[r]^u&F }\;,\]
where \(u\) is an epimorphism, and \(H\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\), the set of the \(U\) above \(X\) such that there exists a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ G\ar@{->>}[r]^v\ar[d]&H_U\ar[d]\\ E_U\ar@{->>}[r]_{u_U}&F_U }\;,\]
with \(G\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\) and \(v\) an epimorphism, is a refinement of \(X\).
If \(\underline S\) is a chaotic site, for example the punctual site (one object, one morphism), we shall omit the adverb "locally" in the preceding definitions.
1.3. Examples

a) Let \(\underline S\) be a punctual site, \(A\) a ring, \(\underline C\) the category of left \(A\)-modules. The full subcategory \(\underline C_0\) of \(\underline C\) formed by the projective (left) \(A\)-modules of finite type is stable under kernels of epimorphisms and liftable in \(\underline C\); an \(A\)-module is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type if and only if it is of finite type in the ordinary sense. The full subcategory \(\underline C_1\) of \(\underline C\) formed by the \(A\)-modules of finite type is quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\) (but not liftable in general); if \(A\) is noetherian, \(\underline C_1\) is stable under kernels of epimorphisms. The full subcategory \(\underline C'_0\) of \(\underline C\) formed by the free \(A\)-modules of finite type is liftable in \(\underline C\) and quasi-stable (not stable in general) under kernels of epimorphisms.

b) Let \((S,\underline{\mathcal O}_S)\) be a space ringed in local rings, \(\underline S\) the site of open sets of \(S\), \(\underline C\) the fibered (and even split (SGA 1 VI 9)) \(\underline S\)-category defined by the functor \(U\longmapsto\) (category of left \(\underline{\mathcal O}_U\)-Modules); \(\underline C\) is a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category (1.1). Let \(\underline C_0\) be the strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) defined by \(U\longmapsto\) (category of free \(\underline{\mathcal O}_U\)-Modules of finite type). An \(\underline{\mathcal O}_S\)-Module \(M\) is locally of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type if and only if \(M\) is of finite type in the ordinary sense (EGA \(0_{\mathrm I}\) 5.2). From (EGA \(0_{\mathrm I}\) 5.2.7 and 5.4.9) it follows that \(\underline C_0\) is locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms and locally liftable in \(\underline C\).

c) Let \(G\) be a group, \(\underline S\) the topos of \(G\)-sets (SGA 4 V 3.11), \(k\) a field (considered as a constant Ring of \(\underline S\)), \(\underline C\) the split \(\underline S\)-category defined by the functor \(U\longmapsto\) (category of left \(k_U\)-Modules); \(\underline C\) is a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category. Let \(\underline C_0\) be the strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) defined by \(U\longmapsto\) (category of free \(k_U\)-Modules of finite type). Since localization in \(\underline S\) amounts to forgetting the operations of \(G\), it is clear that a \(G\)-\(k\)-module \(M\) is locally of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type if and only if \(M\) is of finite dimension as a vector space over \(k\), and that \(\underline C_0\) is locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms and locally liftable in \(\underline C\).
We leave it to the reader to examine the "Galois" variant of c), where \(G\) is a profinite group and \(\underline S\) the topos of sets on which \(G\) operates continuously.
In fact, examples b) and c) are particular cases of the following example:

d) Let \((\underline S,\underline{\mathcal O}_{\underline S})\) be a ringed topos (cf. SGA 4 IV 2.10)(*), and \(\underline C\) the split \(\underline S\)-category defined by \(U\longmapsto\) (category of left \(\underline{\mathcal O}_U\)-Modules); \(\underline C\) is a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category. Let \(\underline C_0\) be the strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) defined by \(U\longmapsto\) (category of free \(\underline{\mathcal O}_U\)-Modules of finite type). An \(\underline{\mathcal O}_{\underline S}\)-Module locally of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type will be said simply to be of finite type. The category \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms and locally liftable in \(\underline C\), as follows from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3.1.

Let \(P\) be an \(\underline{\mathcal O}_{\underline S}\)-Module. Equivalent conditions:

(i) \(P\) is of finite type and the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,\cdot)\) is exact;

(ii) \(P\) is of finite type and every epimorphism \(M\longrightarrow P\) admits locally a section;

(iii) \(P\) is locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type.
The proof is immediate and left as an exercise to the reader.
1.4. An "extension" lemma (cf. EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 11.9.1)

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4.1.

Let \(\underline S\) be a site, \(\underline C\) a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category (1.1), \(\underline C_0\) a strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category (1.1) of \(\underline C\); we assume \(\underline C_0\) locally quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\) (1.2). Let \(S\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(u:E\to F\) be a morphism (of degree 0) of complexes of \(\underline C_S\) (differentials of degree +1), \(G\) the cone of \(u\) (cf. [V] chap. I § 1 2.1), \(n\in\mathbb Z\); we assume that \(H^n(G)\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type (1.2). Then the set of the \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S_{/S}\) satisfying property \(P\) below is a refinement of \(S\).

\(P\): there exist an object \(M\) of \(\underline C_{0X}\) and arrows of \(\underline C_X\)

\[r:M\longrightarrow Z^{n+1}(E_X)=\operatorname{Ker}(E_X^{n+1}\xrightarrow{\;d^{n+1}\;}E_X^{n+2})\]
\[\text{et}\qquad s:M\longrightarrow F_X^n\]

such that the following diagram of \(\underline C_X\)

\[\xymatrix@C=3.5em{ \cdots\ar[r]&E_X^{n-1}\ar[r]^-{\begin{pmatrix}d_{E_X}^{n-1}\\0\end{pmatrix}}\ar[d]_{u_X^{n-1}}&E_X^n\oplus M\ar[r]^-{(d_{E_X}^n,\,r)}\ar[d]_{(u_X^n,\,s)}&E_X^{n+1}\ar[r]\ar[d]^{u_X^{n+1}}&\cdots\\ \cdots\ar[r]&F_X^{n-1}\ar[r]&F_X^n\ar[r]&F_X^{n+1}\ar[r]&\cdots }\]

is a morphism of complexes \(u':E'\longrightarrow F_X\) (i.e. \(d_{F_X}^n\,s=u_X^{n+1}\,r\)), and that

(i) \(H^{n+1}(u')\) is a monomorphism, with \(\operatorname{Im}H^{n+1}(u')=\operatorname{Im}H^{n+1}(u_X)\),

(ii) \(H^n(u')\) is an epimorphism.

Figuratively, one can say that, after locally modifying \(E\) and \(u\), in degree \(n\) only, by adding to \(E^n\) an object of \(\underline C_0\), one can locally make \(H^{n+1}(u)\) injective, without changing its image, and \(H^n(u)\) surjective.

Proof. The plan is as follows: one first shows that one can choose \(r\) and \(s\) locally so as to make \(H^{n+1}(u)\) injective; one checks that the finiteness hypothesis on \(H^n(G)\) is preserved; a new local correction in degree \(n\) then makes it possible to obtain \(H^n(u)\) surjective.
The exact cohomology sequence
\[\longrightarrow H^n(G)\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(E)\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(F)\longrightarrow\]
shows that the kernel of \(H^{n+1}(u)\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type. In other words, the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists an exact sequence
\[M\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(E_X)\xrightarrow{\;H^{n+1}(u)\;}H^{n+1}(F_X)\;,\]

with \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\), is a refinement of \(S\). Since \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\), one may moreover require that the arrow \(M\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(E_X)\) factor through \(Z^{n+1}(E_X)\) as
\[M\xrightarrow{\;r\;}Z^{n+1}(E_X)\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(E_X)\;.\]
The composite arrow
\[M\xrightarrow{\;u_X^{n+1}r\;}Z^{n+1}(F_X)\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(F_X)\]

being zero by construction, \(u_X^{n+1}r\) factors through \(B^{n+1}(F_X)=\operatorname{Im}(F_X^n\xrightarrow{\;d^n\;}F_X^{n+1})\). Applying quasi-liftability once again, one may require that \(u_X^{n+1}r\) factor through \(F_X^n\) as
\[M\xrightarrow{\;s\;}F_X^n\longrightarrow B^{n+1}(F_X)\longrightarrow Z^{n+1}(F_X)\;.\]
One then verifies trivially that \(u'\), defined as in (1.4.1), is a morphism of complexes satisfying (i).
Let \(G'\) be the cone of \(u':E'\longrightarrow F_X\). Let us show that \(H^n(G')\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type. We have, by construction, an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow E_X\xrightarrow{\;v\;}E'\longrightarrow M[-n]\longrightarrow0\;,\]
with \(u'v=u_X\). To compare \(G_X\) and \(G'\), one can, for example, form the "octahedron" of \(K(\underline C_X)\)
\[\xymatrix@C=1.2em@R=1.4em{ &&G_X\ar@/^1.5pc/[dddll]|{+1}&&\\ &&(\ast)&&\\ &M[-n]\ar[uur]&&G'\ar[ll]|{+1}\ar[uul]&\\ E_X\ar[rr]^v&&E'\ar[ul]|{+1}\ar[rr]^{u'}&&F_X\ar[ul]\ar@/_1.5pc/[uuull] }\;.\]

The exact cohomology sequence of the distinguished triangle \((*)\) shows that \(H^n(G_X)\longrightarrow H^n(G')\) is an epimorphism, hence that \(H^n(G')\) is indeed of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type.

The preceding correction thus reduces us to the case where \(H^{n+1}(u)\) is a monomorphism, which we shall now assume. The exact cohomology sequence of \(E\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\) then gives an exact sequence
\[(.)\qquad H^n(E)\xrightarrow{\;H^n(u)\;}H^n(F)\longrightarrow H^n(G)\longrightarrow0\;.\]

Since \(H^n(G)\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type, the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists an epimorphism \(M\longrightarrow H^n(G_X)\) with \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\) is, by definition, a refinement of \(S\). Using the quasi-liftability of \(\underline C_0\) in \(\underline C\), one may require that this epimorphism factor through \(Z^n(F_X)\) as
\[M\xrightarrow{\;s\;}Z^n(F_X)\longrightarrow H^n(F_X)\longrightarrow H^n(G_X)\;.\]

It is clear that \(u'\), defined as in (1.4.1) with (\(r=0\)), is a morphism of complexes and that \(H^{n+1}(u')=H^{n+1}(u_X)\). On the other hand, it is immediate, from the exact sequence \((.)\), that
\[H^n(u'):H^n(E_X)\oplus M\longrightarrow H^n(F_X)\]
is an epimorphism. This completes the proof of (1.4.1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4.2.

Let \(\underline C\) be an abelian category, \(u:E\to F\) a morphism of complexes of \(\underline C\) (resp. an arrow of \(D(\underline C)\)), \(G\) the cone of \(u\). Equivalent conditions, for given \(n\in\mathbb Z\):

(i) \(H^i(G)=0\) for \(i\geq n\);

(ii) \(H^i(u)\) is an isomorphism for \(i>n\) and an epimorphism for \(i=n\).
The proof is immediate from the exact cohomology sequence.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4.3.

We shall say that \(u\) is an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism (resp. an \(n\)-isomorphism) if \(u\) satisfies the equivalent conditions of (1.4.2).
That being so, one deduces at once from (1.4.1) the following

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4.4.

Let \(\underline S\), \(S\), \(\underline C\), \(\underline C_0\) be as in (1.4.1). Let \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) be an \((n+1)\)-quasi-isomorphism of complexes of \(\underline C_S\) (\(n\in\mathbb Z\) given) such that \(H^n(G)\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type. Then the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exist \((M,r,s)\) as in (1.4.1) such that \(u'\) is a \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism is a refinement of \(S\).

Figuratively, one can, given the finiteness condition on \(H^n(G)\), locally "gain one notch", i.e. locally modify \(u\) in degree \(n\) by adding to \(E^n\) an object of \(\underline C_0\) so as to make \(u\) an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4.5.

Let \(\underline C\) be an abelian category, \(n\in\mathbb Z\), and \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism of complexes of \(\underline C\). There exists a commutative diagram of \(C(\underline C)\)

\[\xymatrix{ E\ar[r]^v\ar[d]_u&E'\ar[dl]^{u'}\\ F& }\]

where \(u'\) is a quasi-isomorphism and \(v\) a monomorphism inducing the identity in degrees \(\geq n\) (i.e. such that \(E'^i=E^i\) and \(v^i=\operatorname{Id}(E^i)\) for \(i\geq n\)).
Proof. Applying (1.4.4) in the case where \(\underline S\) is the punctual site and \(\underline C_0=\underline C\), we obtain a commutative triangle of \(C(\underline C)\)
\[\xymatrix{ E\ar[r]^{v_{n-1}}\ar[d]_u&E'_{n-1}\ar[dl]^{u'_{n-1}}\\ F& }\;,\]

where \(u'_{n-1}\) is an \((n-1)\)-quasi-isomorphism and \(v_{n-1}\) a monomorphism inducing the identity in degrees \(\geq n\). Iterating the procedure, one finds, for \(i\leq n-1\), a sequence of commutative triangles of \(C(\underline C)\)
\[\xymatrix{ E'_i\ar[r]^{v_{i-1}}\ar[d]_{u'_i}&E'_{i-1}\ar[dl]^{u'_{i-1}}\\ F& }\;,\]

where \(u'_i\) is an \(i\)-quasi-isomorphism and \(v_{i-1}\) a monomorphism inducing the identity in degrees \(\geq i\). The corollary follows by passage to the limit. Precisely, denoting by \(d'_i\) the differential of \(E'_i\), one defines \(E'\) by
\[E'^i=E^i\;,\quad d'^i=d^i\quad\text{pour }i\geq n\;,\]
\[\text{et}\qquad E'^i=(E'_i)^i\;,\quad d'^i=(d'_i)^i\quad\text{pour }i<n\;,\]
then \(v\) and \(u'\) by
\[v^j=(v_i)^j\;,\quad u'^j=(u'_i)^j\quad\text{pour }i\leq j\;,\]
and one verifies at once that \(E'\), \(v\), \(u'\) thus defined answer the question.
In other words, in brief, one can extend an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism to a quasi-isomorphism "without changing anything of what is given in degrees \(\geq n\)".

footnotes.html: if \(a\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_Y)\) (resp. \(F\in D(\underline C_Y)\)), we shall often write, for short, \(a_X\) instead of \(f^*(a)\).
\(\underline{\mathcal O}_{\underline S}\) is here a ring of \(\underline S\), with unit element, but not necessarily commutative.

blocks.html: 2. Pseudo-coherent complexes

2.0. In this section, \(\underline S\) denotes a site, \(S\) an object of \(\underline S\), \(\underline C\) a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category (1.1), \(\underline C_0\) a strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\). We assume that \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms (1.2) and locally quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\) (1.2).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.

Let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(E\) be a complex of \(\underline C_X\), and \(n\in\mathbb Z\). We shall say that \(E\) is strictly \(\underline C_0\)-\(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent, resp. \(\underline C_0\)-perfect) if \(E^i=0\) for \(i\) large enough and \(E^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\) for \(i\geq n\) (resp. for every \(i\), resp. for every \(i\) and \(E^i=0\) for almost every \(i\)).
When there is no fear of confusion, we shall sometimes say "strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp...)" instead of "strictly \(\underline C_0\)-\(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp...)".

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.1.

Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}C(\underline C_S)\) and \(n\in\mathbb Z\). If \(E\) is strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent and if \(H^i(E)=0\) for \(i>n\), then \(H^n(E)\) is of finite type.
Proof. The sequence
\[0\longrightarrow Z^n(E)\longrightarrow E^n\longrightarrow E^{n+1}\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow0\]

is exact and \(E^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\) for \(i\geq n\). Since \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms, it follows that \(Z^n(E)\) is of finite type, hence so is \(H^n(E)\), which is a quotient of it.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\) and \(n\in\mathbb Z\). Then:

a) If \(F\) is strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (relatively to \(\underline C_0\)) and if \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) is a quasi-isomorphism, the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(v:F'\longrightarrow E_X\) with \(F'\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent is a refinement of \(S\).

b) The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(u:E\longrightarrow F_X\) of \(C(\underline C_X)\), with \(E\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, is a refinement of \(S\);

\(i'\) same condition as (i), but with \(u\) an isomorphism of \(D(\underline C_X)\);

(ii) the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism (1.4.3) \(u:E\longrightarrow F_X\) of \(C(\underline C_X)\), with \(E\) strictly perfect, is a refinement of \(S\);

(ii') same condition as (ii), but with \(u\) an \(n\)-isomorphism of \(D(\underline C_X)\).

Proof. a) There exists, for a sufficiently large integer \(i\), an \(i\)-quasi-isomorphism \(v_i:F'_i\longrightarrow E\), with \(F'_i\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (take \(F'_i=0\)). Let us show that, if \(i>n\), there exists, after possibly refining \(S\), an \((i-1)\)-quasi-isomorphism \(v_{i-1}:F'_{i-1}\longrightarrow E\), with \(F'_{i-1}\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. By (1.4.4), it suffices to show that \(H^{i-1}(M')\), where \(M'\) is the cone of \(v_i\), is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type. Now, if \(M''\) denotes the cone of \(uv_i\), the hypothesis that \(u\) is a quasi-isomorphism and (1.4.2) imply that we have
\[H^j(M')\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}H^j(M'')\qquad\text{pour tout }j\]
\[\text{et}\qquad H^j(M')=0\quad\text{pour }j\geq i.\]

The cone formula \(M''^j=F_i'^{j+1}\oplus F^j\) shows on the other hand that \(M''\) is strictly \((i-1)\)-pseudo-coherent. Hence, by virtue of (2.1.1), \(H^{i-1}(M'')=H^{i-1}(M')\) is of finite type, whence our assertion. One deduces from this, by iteration, and after possibly refining \(S\), an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism \(v_n:F'_n\longrightarrow E\), where \(F'_n\) is strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. But, by (1.4.5), one can extend \(v_n\) to a quasi-isomorphism \(v:F'\longrightarrow E\) without changing anything in degrees \(\geq n\), hence in particular with \(F'\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, which proves a).

b) The equivalence of conditions (i) and \(i'\) follows at once from a) and from the definition of the isomorphisms of \(D^-(\underline C_X)\). If \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline C_S)\) is strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, there exists an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow0\;,\]

where \(E'\) is strictly perfect, and \(E''^i=0\) for \(i\geq n\); consequently \(E'\longrightarrow E\) is an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism, whence the implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii). The implication (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii') being trivial, it remains to prove (ii') \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). So let \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) be an \(n\)-isomorphism of \(D^-(\underline C_S)\), with \(E\) strictly perfect; \(u\) is thus defined by a diagram of \(C^-(\underline C_S)\)
\[\xymatrix@C=1em{ &E'\ar[dl]_s\ar[dr]^{u'}&\\ E&&F }\;,\]

where \(s\) is a quasi-isomorphism and \(u'\) an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism. By virtue of a), there exists, after possibly refining \(S\), a quasi-isomorphism \(t:E''\longrightarrow E'\), with \(E''\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. One then applies (1.4.5) to extend \(u't:E''\longrightarrow F\) to a quasi-isomorphism \(E'''\longrightarrow F\) with \(E'''\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, which proves (ii') \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) and completes the proof of (2.2).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.

Under the hypotheses of (2.2), we shall say that \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\) is \(n\)-\(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent if \(F\) satisfies the equivalent conditions b) of (2.2). We shall say that \(F\) is \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent if \(F\) is \(n\)-\(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent for every \(n\).

When this cannot lead to confusion, we shall sometimes allow ourselves to say \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, pseudo-coherent, instead of \(n\)-\(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent, \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.3.1.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\) be a pseudo-coherent complex. The set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(E\longrightarrow F_X\) with \(E\) strictly pseudo-coherent (2.1) is not in general a refinement of \(S\). However, we shall see later (2.7. a) and Exp. II) important cases where this is so.
The proof of (2.2 a)) gives the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4.

Let \(n,i\in\mathbb Z\), and let \(u:E\to F\) be an \(i\)-quasi-isomorphism of \(C(\underline C_S)\), with \(F\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent and \(E\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. Then, after possibly refining \(S\), there exists a commutative triangle of \(C(\underline C_S)\)

\[\xymatrix{ E\ar[r]^v\ar[d]_u&E'\ar[dl]^{u'}\\ F& }\;,\]

where \(E'\) is strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, \(u'\) a quasi-isomorphism and \(v\) a monomorphism inducing the identity in degrees \(\geq i\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.5.

a) If \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent), \(F[m]\) is \((n-m)\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent).

b) Let

\[(*)\qquad\xymatrix{ &G\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ E\ar[rr]&&F\ar[ul] }\]

be a distinguished triangle of \(D(\underline C_S)\). If \(E\) and \(G\) are \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent), then \(F\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent).
Proof. a) Trivial from the definitions.
b) It suffices, of course, to prove the non-resp. assertion. The triangle \((*)\) can also be written
\[\xymatrix{ &F\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ G[-1]\ar[rr]&&E\ar[ul] }\;.\]

By a), \(G[-1]\) is \((n+1)\)-pseudo-coherent. One is thus reduced to proving that, if \(E\) is \((n+1)\)-pseudo-coherent and \(F\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, then \(G\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. After localizing if necessary, one may assume \(E\) (resp. \(F\)) strictly \((n+1)\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \(n\)-pseudo-coherent). The arrow \(E\xrightarrow{\;u\;}F\) of \(D^-(\underline C_S)\) is defined by a pair of arrows of \(C^-(\underline C_S)\)
\[\xymatrix@C=1em{ &E'\ar[dl]_s\ar[dr]^{u'}&\\ E&&F }\;,\]

where \(s\) is a quasi-isomorphism. By (2.2 a)), there exists, after localizing if necessary, a quasi-isomorphism \(t:E''\longrightarrow E'\), where \(E''\) is strictly \((n+1)\)-pseudo-coherent. One is thus reduced to the case where \(u\) is defined by a "true" arrow of \(C^-(\underline C_S)\). The assertion then follows from the definitions and from the formula \(C(u)^i=E^{i+1}\oplus F^i\), where \(C(u)\) denotes the cone of \(u\).

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 2.6.

By "rotation" of the triangles, one sees that pseudo-coherence properties on two of the objects of the triangle \((*)\) imply another one on the third. One can summarize the various cases in the table below (the small letters denote degrees of pseudo-coherence and each row corresponds to an implication whose target is underlined):

\[\begin{array}{ccc} E&F&G\\ n+1&n&\underline{n}\\ n&\underline{n}&n\\ \underline{n}&n&n-1 \end{array}\;.\]

We shall denote by \(D(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-}n\text{-coh}}\) (resp. \(D(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\)) the strictly full fibered subcategory of \(D(\underline C)\) whose fibre at each \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) is formed by the objects of \(D(\underline C_X)\) which are \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) relatively to \(\underline C_0\). The category \(D(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\) is, by virtue of (2.5), a triangulated sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(D(\underline C)\) (1.1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7

Suppose that \(\underline S\) is a punctual site, hence \(\underline C=\underline C_S\), \(\underline C_0=\underline C_{0S}\). Then:

a) If \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C)\) is pseudo-coherent, there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(E\longrightarrow F\), with \(E\) strictly pseudo-coherent (2.1).

b) Denote by \(K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline C_0)\) the full triangulated subcategory of \(K^-(\underline C_0)\) formed by the acyclic complexes. The canonical functor

\[K^-(\underline C_0)/K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow D(\underline C)\]

is fully faithful and has essential image \(D(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\).

Proof. a) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\), and suppose constructed an \(i\)-quasi-isomorphism (of \(C(\underline C)\)) \(u_i:E_i\longrightarrow F\) with \(E_i\) strictly pseudo-coherent (this is possible for \(i\) large enough with \(E_i=0\)). Let \(G_i\) be the cone of \(u_i\). It follows from (1.4.2) and (2.1.1) that \(H^{i-1}(G_i)\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type. Hence, by virtue of (1.4.4), there exists a commutative triangle of \(C(\underline C)\)
\[\xymatrix{ E_i\ar[r]^{v_{i-1}}\ar[d]_{u_i}&E_{i-1}\ar[dl]^{u_{i-1}}\\ F& }\;,\]

where \(E_{i-1}\) is strictly pseudo-coherent, \(u_{i-1}\) an \((i-1)\)-quasi-isomorphism, and \(v_{i-1}\) a monomorphism inducing the identity in degrees \(\geq i\). One deduces from this by passage to the limit (cf. proof of (1.4.5)) a quasi-isomorphism \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) with \(E\) strictly pseudo-coherent.
b) Apply the criterion ([V] chap I § 2 4.2 b)(i)) and a).
Note that the proof of a) gives the following more precise result, which generalizes (1.4.5):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7.1.

Under the hypotheses of (2.7), let \(u:E\longrightarrow F\) be an \(n\)-quasi-isomorphism of \(C(\underline C)\), with \(F\) pseudo-coherent and \(E\) strictly pseudo-coherent. There exists then a commutative triangle of \(C(\underline C)\)

\[\xymatrix{ E\ar[r]^v\ar[d]_u&E'\ar[dl]^{u'}\\ F& }\;,\]

where \(E'\) is strictly pseudo-coherent, \(u'\) a quasi-isomorphism, and \(v\) a monomorphism inducing the identity in degrees \(\geq n\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.8.

Let \(n\in\mathbb N\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_S\). We shall say that \(F\) is of finite \(n\)-\(\underline C_0\)-presentation if the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists an exact sequence

\[E^{-n}\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E^0\longrightarrow F_X\longrightarrow0\;,\]

with \(E^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\) for \(-n\leq i\leq0\), is a refinement of \(S\). We shall say that \(F\) is of finite \(\infty\)-\(\underline C_0\)-presentation if \(F\) is of finite \(n\)-\(\underline C_0\)-presentation for every \(n\in\mathbb N\).
Of course, we shall sometimes say simply finite \(n\)-presentation when there is no possible ambiguity about \(\underline C_0\).
We shall say "of finite presentation" for "of finite 1-presentation", and "of finite type" for "of finite 0-presentation", this last notion coinciding with the one already introduced in (1.2).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.9.

Let \(n\in\mathbb N\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_S\). Equivalent conditions:

(i) \(F\) is of finite \(n\)-presentation (resp. of finite \(\infty\)-presentation);

(ii) \(F\) is \((-n)\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent).

In the case where \(\underline S\) is a punctual site, \(F\) is pseudo-coherent if and only if there exists an exact sequence

\[\cdots\longrightarrow E^i\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E^0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0\;,\]

with \(E^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\) for every \(i\).

Proof. For the equivalence (i)\(\Longleftrightarrow\)(ii), it evidently suffices to treat the non-resp. case. The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) is immediate from the definitions, and the implication (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) follows at once from (2.4): the arrow \(0\longrightarrow F\) is a 1-quasi-isomorphism, and one deduces from it (after localizing if necessary) a quasi-isomorphism \(E\longrightarrow F\) with \(E\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent and \(E^i=0\) for \(i\geq1\), i.e. a finite \(n\)-presentation of \(F\). The second assertion is proved in an analogous way \(utiliser (2.7.1)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.10.

a) Let \(m,n\in\mathbb Z\), with \(n\leq m\). If \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline C_S)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degrees \(\geq m\), there exists locally a quasi-isomorphism \(E\longrightarrow F\), with \(E\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent and \(E^i=0\) for \(i\geq m\). If \(\underline S\) is punctual, and \(F\) is pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degrees \(\geq m\), there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(E\longrightarrow F\) with \(E\) strictly pseudo-coherent and \(E^i=0\) for \(i\geq m\).

b) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline C_S)\) be acyclic in degrees \(>n\). For \(F\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H^n(F)\) be of finite type.
Proof. a) Since \(F\) is acyclic in degrees \(\geq m\), the arrow \(0\longrightarrow F\) is an \(m\)-quasi-isomorphism. The assertion follows from (2.4) and (2.7.1).
b) There exists a distinguished triangle of \(D^-(\underline C_S)\):
\[\xymatrix{ &F'\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ F\ar[rr]&&H^n(F)[-n]\ar[ul] }\;,\]

(to see this, begin by truncating \(F\), replacing \(F^n\) by \(Z^n(F)\) and \(F^i\) by 0 for \(i>n\)). The exact cohomology sequence implies \(H^i(F')=0\) for \(i\geq n-1\), hence \(F'\) is \((n-1)\)-pseudo-coherent. One concludes thanks to (2.5) and (2.6).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.11.

Let \(n\in\mathbb Z\).

a) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}C^-(\underline C_S)\). If, for every \(i\geq n\) (resp. for every \(i\)), \(F^i\) is of finite \((i-n)\)-presentation (resp. of finite \(\infty\)-presentation), then \(F\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent).

b) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline C_S)\). If, for every \(i\geq n\) (resp. for every \(i\)), \(H^i(F)\) is of finite \((i-n)\)-presentation (resp. of finite \(\infty\)-presentation), then \(F\) is pseudo-coherent.
Proof. It suffices to treat the non-resp. assertions.
a) One argues by induction on \(N\) such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i>n+N\). If \(N=0\), the assertion follows at once from (2.10 b)). Suppose \(N\geq1\). We have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow F^{n+N}[-n-N]\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow0\;,\]

By the induction hypothesis, \(F'\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent; on the other hand, \(F^{n+N}[-n-N]\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent by hypothesis (2.9 and 2.5.a)); hence, by virtue of (2.5.b)), \(F\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent.

b) One argues by induction on \(N\) such that \(H^i(F)=0\) for \(i>n+N\). If \(N=0\), the assertion follows from (2.10 b)). Suppose \(N\geq1\). We have a distinguished triangle of \(D^-(\underline C_S)\) (cf. proof of (2.10.b)):
\[\xymatrix{ &F'\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ F\ar[rr]&&H^{n+N}(F)[-n-N]\ar[ul] }\;.\]
The exact cohomology sequence implies
\[H^i(F')=0\quad\text{pour }i\geq n+N-1,\text{ i.e. }i>(n-1)+(N-1),\]
\[\text{et}\qquad H^i(F')\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}H^{i+1}(F)\quad\text{pour }i<n+N-1\;.\]

By the induction hypothesis (applied with \(n-1\) instead of \(n\)), \(F'\) is therefore \((n-1)\)-pseudo-coherent. On the other hand, \(H^{n+N}(F)[-n-N]\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (2.9 and 2.5.a)). Hence, by virtue of (2.6), \(F\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.11.1.

If \(F\) is pseudo-coherent, the \(F^i\) (resp. \(H^i(F)\)) are not in general pseudo-coherent, nor even of finite type. However, we shall see in the next paragraph, in the sorites on the notions of coherence, important examples where the pseudo-coherence of \(F\) implies that of the \(H^i(F)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.12.

Let \(F',F''\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\), and \(F=F'+F''\). For \(F\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) it is necessary and sufficient that \(F'\) and \(F''\) be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent).

Proof. It suffices to prove the non-resp. assertion. Sufficiency is evident; let us prove necessity. One may assume, by induction, that it has already been shown that \(F'\) and \(F''\) are \((n+1)\)-pseudo-coherent. After localizing if necessary, one may even assume that \(F'\) and \(F''\) are strictly \((n+1)\)-pseudo-coherent and that \(F=F'+F''\) in \(C^-(\underline C_S)\). Denote by \(F'_{>n}\) (resp. \(F'_{\leq n}\)) the complex deduced from \(F'\) by replacing \(F'^i\) by 0 for \(i\leq n\) (resp. \(i>n\)), and similarly \(F''_{>n}\) (resp. \(F''_{\leq n}\))... We have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow F'_{>n}+F''_{>n}\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F'_{\leq n}+F''_{\leq n}\longrightarrow0\;.\]

\(F'_{>n}+F''_{>n}\) is strictly perfect and \(F\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, hence (2.6) \(F'_{\leq n}+F''_{\leq n}\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. This means, by (2.10 b)), that \(H^n(F'_{\leq n}+F''_{\leq n})\) is of finite type. But we have
\[H^n(F'_{\leq n}+F''_{\leq n})=H^n(F'_{\leq n})+H^n(F''_{\leq n})\;,\]

hence \(H^n(F'_{\leq n})\) (resp. \(H^n(F''_{\leq n})\)) is of finite type, hence again thanks to (2.10.b)) \(F'_{\leq n}\)) (resp. \(F''_{\leq n}\)) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. Applying (2.5) to the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow F'_{>n}\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow F'_{\leq n}\longrightarrow0\;,\]
(resp....), one deduces that \(F'\) (resp. \(F''\)) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, qed.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.13.

Let \((\underline S',\underline C',\underline C'_0)\) be a trio satisfying the same hypotheses (2.0) as \((\underline S,\underline C,\underline C_0)\). Let \(f:\underline S'\longrightarrow\underline S\) be a morphism of sites ([G] I 1.4), corresponding to an underlying functor \(f^{-1}:\underline S\longrightarrow\underline S'\), and let \(u:D^*(\underline C)\longrightarrow D^*(\underline C')\) (\(*=+,-,b\)) be a cartesian functor above \(f\), i.e. a cartesian \(\underline S\)-functor \(D^*(\underline C')\times_{\underline S'}\underline S\longleftarrow D^*(\underline C)\). We assume that \(u\) is exact on each fibre and that there exist \(a,b\in\mathbb Z\) such that the following conditions are satisfied for every \(S\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\):

(i) if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0S}\), \(u(E)\) is \(a\)-\(\underline C'_0\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \(\underline C'_0\)-pseudo-coherent)

(ii) if \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C_S)\) is acyclic in degrees \(>0\), \(u(F)\) is acyclic in degrees \(>b\).

Then, if \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C_S)\) is \(m\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(\underline C_0\) and acyclic in degrees \(>n\), \(u(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C'_{S'})\) (where \(S'=f^{-1}(S)\)) is \(\sup(m+b,n+a)\)-pseudo-coherent (rel. to \(\underline C'_0\)) (resp. \((m+b)\)-pseudo-coherent) and acyclic in degrees \(>n+b\).

In particular, in the resp. case, if \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C_S)\) is pseudo-coherent (rel. to \(\underline C_0\)), \(u(F)\) is pseudo-coherent (rel. to \(\underline C'_0\)).

Proof. One may restrict to the non-resp. case. Let us first show that if \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C_S)\) is strictly perfect (2.1) and such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i>n\), then \(u(F)\) is \((n+a)\)-\(\underline C'_0\)-pseudo-coherent. Let us argue by induction on the number \(N\) of indices \(i\) such that \(F^i\neq0\). If \(N=1\), we have \(F=F^p[-p]\) for some \(p\leq n\), hence \(u(F)\) is \((p+a)\)-\(\underline C'_0\)-pseudo-coherent, hence a fortiori \((n+a)\)-pseudo-coherent. If \(N>1\), we have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F''\longrightarrow0\;,\]
where \(F'\) and \(F''\) are strictly perfect of length \(\leq N-1\) and such that \(F'^i=F''^i=0\) for \(i>n\). Applying (2.5.b)) to the distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &u(F'')\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ u(F')\ar[rr]&&u(F)\ar[ul] }\;,\]
one deduces from the induction hypothesis that \(u(F)\) is \((n+a)\)-pseudo-coherent.

Let us now pass to the general case: \(F\) \(m\)-\(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degrees \(>n\). After localizing if necessary, one may, thanks to (2.10.a)), assume \(F\) strictly \(m\)-pseudo-coherent and such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i>n\). We then have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F''\longrightarrow0\;,\]
where \(F''\) is zero in degrees \(\geq m\) and \(F'\) is strictly perfect and zero in degrees \(>n\). In the distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &u(F'')\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ u(F')\ar[rr]&&u(F)\ar[ul] }\;,\]
\(u(F'')\) is acyclic in degrees \(\geq m+b\), hence \((m+b)\)-pseudo-coherent, and \(u(F')\) is \((n+a)\)-pseudo-coherent by what we have seen. One concludes by (2.5.b)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.14.

Let \(\underline C_1\) be a strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) such that \(\underline C_0\subset\underline C_1\subset\underline C\). We assume that every object of \(\underline C_1\) is \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent. Then:

(i) \(\underline C_1\) is locally quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\) (1.2) and locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms (1.2).

(ii) Let \(S\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\). For \(F\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) relatively to \(\underline C_1\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be so relatively to \(\underline C_0\).

Proof. (i) Let \(E\to F\) be an epimorphism of \(\underline C_S\), with \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{1S}\). Since \(F\) is \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent (hence a fortiori of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type), there exists (after localizing if necessary) an epimorphism \(E'\longrightarrow F\) with \(E'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0S}\). Using the quasi-liftability of \(\underline C_0\) in \(\underline C\), one may assume (after localizing again) that the latter factors through \(E\), whence the quasi-liftability of \(\underline C_1\). On the other hand, if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_S\) admits a finite right resolution by objects of \(\underline C_1\), \(E\) is \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent by virtue of (2.11.a)), hence a fortiori of finite \(\underline C_1\)-type.

(ii) It suffices to prove necessity. For this one may apply, at one's choice, (2.11.a)) or (2.13) with \(\underline S'=\underline S\), \(\underline C'=\underline C\), \(f=\) the Identity, \(u=\) the Identity, \(\underline C'_0\) and \(\underline C_0\) replaced respectively by \(\underline C_0\) and \(\underline C_1\)).

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 2.14.1.

There thus exists a largest strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) giving the same notion of \(n\)-pseudo-coherence (resp. pseudo-coherence) as \(\underline C_0\), namely the category formed by the \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent objects. The latter is locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 2.15.

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos and \(\underline C\), \(\underline C_0\) the \(\underline S\)-categories considered in (1.3.d)). The fibered category \(D(\underline C)\) will often be denoted \(D(\underline S,A)\), or \(D(_A\underline S)\) (to suggest that left \(A\)-Modules are involved), or even \(D(\underline S)\) if there is no ambiguity about \(A\). We shall denote by \(S\) the final object of \(\underline S\) and set \(D(\underline C_S)=D(_AS)\) (or \(D(S)\)). An object of \(D(S)\) will be said to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) if it is \(n\)-\(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \(\underline C_0\)-pseudo-coherent) (likewise for the strict notions). The fibered category \(D(\underline C)_{n\text{-}\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\) (resp. \(D(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-coh}}\)) (2.6) will be denoted \(D(_A\underline S)_{n\text{-coh}}\) (resp. \(D(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\)). One will observe that by virtue of (2.14) one obtains the same notion of pseudo-coherence if one replaces \(\underline C_0\) by the \(\underline S\)-category \(\underline C_1\) defined by \(U\longmapsto\) (category of left \(A_U\)-Modules which are locally direct factors of free Modules of finite type). If an \(A\)-Module \(M\) is a direct factor of a free \(A\)-Module of finite type, it does not follow, in general, that \(M\) is locally free of finite type. We do have, however:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.15.1.

Suppose that \(\underline S\) satisfies one of the following two hypotheses:

(i) \(\underline S\) "has enough points" (SGA 4 IV 6.4) and for every point \(s\) of \(\underline S\) the projective (left) \(A_s\)-modules of finite type are free, this last condition being satisfied for example (BOURBAKI Alg. Comm. chap. II § 3 no 2 cor.2 of prop. 5) if \(A_s/\mathrm{radical}(A_s)\) is a field or if \(\mathrm{radical}(A_s)\) is nilpotent.

(ii) \((\underline S,A)\) is a "locally ringed" topos in the sense of [1], which means that \(A\) is commutative and that for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) and every \(f\in\Gamma(X,A)\) we have \(X=X_f\cup X_{1-f}\), where \(X_f\) (resp. \(X_{1-f}\)) denotes the largest subobject of \(X\) on which \(f\) (resp. \(1-f\)) is invertible.

Then every direct factor of a free (left) \(A\)-module of finite type is locally free of finite type.
Proof. Assume (i). One first remarks that if \(E\) and \(F\) are two \(A\)-Modules, with \(E\) of finite presentation, the canonical arrow
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)_s\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(E_s,F_s)\]

is an isomorphism for every point \(s\) of \(\underline S\). It follows that if \(E\) and \(F\) are both of finite presentation, and if \(E_s\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}F_s\) at a point \(s\) of \(\underline S\), then there exists a \(s\)-pointed object \(U\) of \(\underline S\) (*) such that \(E|U\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}F|U\). The conclusion follows at once.

Assume (ii). Let \(n\in\mathbb N\), and denote by \(\mathbb D^n\) the functor on the category of locally ringed topoi (belonging to a fixed universe...) defined by \(T\longmapsto\Gamma(T,\underline{\mathcal O}_T)^n\). It is known [1] that the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{End}}_{\mathbb D}(\mathbb D^n)\) is represented by \(M_n=\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb Z[T_{ij}]\) (\(1\leq i\leq n\), \(1\leq j\leq n\)) equipped with the endomorphism \(U_n\) of \((\underline{\mathcal O}_{M_n})^n\) defined by the matrix \((T_{ij})\). Now let \(p:A^n\longrightarrow A^n\) be a projector; let us show that \(p(A^n)\) is locally free of finite type. By what has just been said, there exists a morphism \(s:\underline S\longrightarrow M_n\) of locally ringed topoi such that \(s^*(u_n)=p\). One can interpret \(s\) as a point of \(M_n\) with values in \(\Gamma(S,A)\), i.e. a matrix of order \(n\) with coefficients in \(\Gamma(S,A)\); since the latter is a projector, \(s\) factors through the closed subscheme \(i:P_n\to M_n\) corresponding to the projectors in \(\underline S\xrightarrow{\;s'\;}P_n\xrightarrow{\;i\;}M_n\). We therefore have \(p=s'^*i^*(u_n)\), and we are done, for by the assertion under hypothesis (i), the image of \(i^*(u_n)\) is a locally free sheaf of finite type.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.16.

Let \(f:(\underline S',A')\longrightarrow(\underline S,A)\) be a morphism of ringed topoi (defined by a functor \(f^{-1}:\underline S\longrightarrow\underline S'\) and a morphism of sheaves of rings \(f^{-1}(A)\longrightarrow A'\)). Denote by \(D(_{A'}\underline S'_A)\) the derived category of that of \(A'\)-\(A\)-biModules (left over \(A'\), right over \(A\)), and

\[\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\;:\;D^-(_{A'}\underline S'_A)\times_{\underline S'}D^-(_A\underline S)\longrightarrow D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\]

the derived functor of the tensor product \((E',E)\longmapsto E'\otimes_{f^{-1}(A)}f^{-1}(E)\).

Let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(X'=u(X)\), \(E'\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_{A'}X'_A)\), \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_AX)\). Then if \(E'\) is \(m'\)-pseudo-coherent (as an object of \(D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\)) and acyclic in degrees \(>n'\) and if \(E\) is \(m\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degrees \(>n\), \(E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}E\) is \(\sup(m+n',m'+n)\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degrees \(>n+n'\).

Proof. After replacing \(\underline S\) by \(\underline S_{/X}\) if necessary, one may assume \(X=S\), and one applies (2.13) with \(u=E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}:D^-(_A\underline S)\to D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\). Let \(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(U'=f^{-1}(U)\), \(E\) be a complex of left \(A_U\)-Modules. If \(E\) is acyclic in degrees \(>n\), \(E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}E\) is acyclic in degrees \(>n+n'\) (definition of \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\)); on the other hand if \(E=(A_U)^p\), we have \(E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}E=(E'|U')^p\), which is \(m'\)-pseudo-coherent by hypothesis (and (2.12)). The conditions of (2.13) are thus indeed satisfied, with \(a=m'\), \(b=n'\), whence the conclusion.
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.16.1.

Under the hypotheses of (2.16), we have:

a) The functor \(\mathbf Lf^*:D^-(_A\underline S)\to D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\) (which is none other than the functor \(A'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}\)) induces a functor

\[\mathbf Lf^*:D^-(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^-(_{A'}\underline S')_{\mathrm{coh}}\]

(notation of (2.6)).

b) If \(A\) is commutative, the functor \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A:D^-(\underline S)\times_{\underline S}D^-(\underline S)\longrightarrow D^-(\underline S)\) induces a functor

\[\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\;:\;D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\times_{\underline S}D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\;.\]

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 2.16.2.

Let \(\underline S\) be a topos, and \(A\longrightarrow A'\) a morphism of Rings of \(\underline S\). One makes one of the following hypotheses:

(i) \(A\longrightarrow A'\) is surjective with kernel a nilpotent Ideal

(ii) the functor \(A'\otimes_A-\) on the category of left \(A\)-modules is exact and faithful.

Show that \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_A\underline S)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) if and only if \(A'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_{A'}\underline S)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent).

Hints: First show that a left \(A\)-Module \(L\) is of finite type if and only if \(A'\otimes_AL\) is (cf. Bourbaki, Alg. commutative, chap. I, § 3, prop. 11 and chap. II, § 3, prop. 4), then reduce to this case by truncation, using (2.5) and (2.10 b)).

footnotes.html: i.e. such that \(s\) factors through \(\underline S_{/U}\).

pages.title: 3. Link with the classical notion of coherence

blocks.html: 3. Link with the classical notion of coherence
3.0. The hypotheses and notations are those of (2.0), except that we no longer assume \(\underline C_0\) locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_S\). We shall say that \(F\) is precoherent (relatively to \(\underline C_0\)) (*) if, for every \(U\) above \(S\) and every morphism \(u:E\to F|U\) where \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0U}\), \(\operatorname{Ker}(u)\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-type (1.2). We shall say that \(F\) is coherent (relatively to \(\underline C_0\)) if \(F\) is precoherent and of finite type.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.2.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_S\). It is clear from the definitions that if \(F\) is precoherent (resp. of finite type) then every subobject (resp. quotient) of \(F\) is precoherent (resp. of finite type).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

a) Let

\[0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow H\]

be an exact sequence of \(\underline C_S\). If \(G\) is of finite type and \(H\) precoherent, \(F\) is of finite type.

b) Let

\[F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow H\longrightarrow0\]

be an exact sequence of \(\underline C_S\). If \(F\) is of finite type and \(G\) precoherent, \(H\) is precoherent.

c) Let

\[0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow H\longrightarrow0\]

be an exact sequence of \(\underline C_S\). If \(F\) and \(H\) are precoherent (resp. of finite type), so is \(G\).
Proof. a) After localizing if necessary, we have an epimorphism \(G'\longrightarrow G\) with \(G'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\). Whence a commutative diagram with exact rows
\[\xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&F'\ar[r]\ar[d]&G'\ar[r]\ar[d]&H\ar[d]^{\mathrm{Id}}\\ 0\ar[r]&F\ar[r]&G\ar[r]&H }\;.\]
\(F'\) is of finite type since \(H\) is precoherent, but \(F'\longrightarrow F\) is an epimorphism, hence \(F\) is of finite type.

b) Let \(U\) be above \(S\), and \(H'\xrightarrow{\;f\;}H|U\) a morphism of \(\underline C_U\) with \(H'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0U}\). After a change of notation, one may assume \(U=S\). Thanks to the quasi-liftability of \(\underline C_0\) in \(\underline C\), there exists (after localizing if necessary) a commutative square
\[\xymatrix{ G'\ar@{->>}[r]^p\ar[d]&H'\ar[d]^f\\ G\ar@{->>}[r]&H }\;,\]

where the horizontal arrows are epimorphisms and \(G'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\). Since \(\operatorname{Ker}(f)\) is a quotient of \(\operatorname{Ker}(fp)\), it suffices to prove that \(\operatorname{Ker}(fp)\) is of finite type, so one may assume that \(H'=G'\). The arrows \(F\to G\) and \(H'\to G\) define \(g:F\oplus H'\longrightarrow G\). Setting \(K=\operatorname{Ker}(G\to H)\), we thus have a commutative diagram, in which the rows are exact and \(q\) is an epimorphism:
\[\xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&F\ar[r]\ar[d]_q&F\oplus H'\ar[r]\ar[d]^g&H'\ar[r]\ar[d]^f&0\\ 0\ar[r]&K\ar[r]&G\ar[r]&H\ar[r]&0 }\;.\]

The snake diagram shows that \(\operatorname{Ker}(g)\to\operatorname{Ker}(f)\) is an epimorphism. But, since \(F\oplus H'\) is of finite type and \(G\) precoherent, \(\operatorname{Ker}(g)\) is of finite type by a), and we are done.

c) Let us first prove the non-resp. assertion. Up to a change of notation, the point is to show that if \(g:G'\longrightarrow G\) is a morphism of \(\underline C_S\) with \(G'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\), then \(\operatorname{Ker}(g)\) is of finite type. Denoting by \(H'\) the image of the composite arrow \(G'\longrightarrow H\), we have a commutative diagram with exact rows
\[\xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&F'\ar[r]\ar[d]_f&G'\ar[r]\ar[d]^g&H'\ar[r]\ar[d]^h&0\\ 0\ar[r]&F\ar[r]&G\ar[r]&H\ar[r]&0 }\;.\]

Since \(h\) is a monomorphism, the snake diagram gives an isomorphism \(\operatorname{Ker}(f)\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\operatorname{Ker}(g)\). But \(F'\) is of finite type by a), hence (again thanks to a)) \(\operatorname{Ker}(f)\) is of finite type, which proves the non-resp. assertion. Let us pass to the resp. assertion (strictly speaking one cannot invoke (2.5 b)) since we do not assume that \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms). After localizing if necessary, there exist epimorphisms \(F'\xrightarrow{\;u\;}F\), \(H'\xrightarrow{\;v\;}H\), with \(F'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\), \(H'\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\). After localizing further, one may assume that \(v\) factors through \(G\), whence a commutative diagram with exact rows
\[\xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&F'\ar[r]\ar@{->>}[d]_u&F'\oplus H'\ar[r]\ar[d]^w&H'\ar[r]\ar@{->>}[d]^v&0\\ 0\ar[r]&F\ar[r]&G\ar[r]&H\ar[r]&0 }\;.\]
Since \(u\) and \(v\) are epimorphisms, so is \(w\), and we are done. This completes the proof of (3.3).
One immediately deduces from this the following

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4.

The (strictly) full subcategory of \(\underline C_S\) formed by the coherent objects is stable under finite projective (resp. inductive) limits. In particular the kernel, the cokernel, the image of an arrow between two coherent objects are coherent. Let

\[E^0\longrightarrow E^1\longrightarrow E^2\longrightarrow E^3\longrightarrow E^4\]

be an exact sequence of \(\underline C_S\). If \(E^0\), \(E^1\), \(E^3\), \(E^4\) are coherent, so is \(E^2\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5.

Suppose that the objects of \(\underline C_0\) are \(\underline C_0\)-coherent (which implies in particular that \(\underline C_0\) is locally quasi-stable under kernels of epimorphisms, i.e. that we are under the conditions of (2.0)).

a) Equivalent conditions on \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_S\):

(i) \(F\) is of finite presentation;

(ii) \(F\) is coherent;

(iii) \(F\) is of finite \(\infty\)-presentation (2.8).

b) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline C_S)\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\). We have:

\[F\ n\text{-pseudo-cohérent}\Longleftrightarrow\begin{array}{l}H^i(F)\text{ cohérent pour }i\geq n+1\;,\\ \text{et }H^n(F)\text{ de type fini}\end{array}\;.\]

In particular, for \(F\) to be pseudo-coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H^n(F)\) be coherent for every \(n\).
Proof. Assertion a) and the implication \(\Longrightarrow\) of b) follow trivially from (3.4). The implication \(\Longleftarrow\) of b) follows at once from a) and (2.11.b)).

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 3.6.

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(\underline C\), \(\underline C_0\) the \(\underline S\)-categories of (1.3.d)) (cf. also (2.15)). For the objects of \(\underline C_0\) to be coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(A\) be coherent (i.e. precoherent). Here are examples where this is so:

– \(A\) is constant with value a left noetherian ring (SGA 4 IX 2.1).

– \(\underline S\) is the topos of sheaves of sets (for the Zariski topology) on a locally noetherian scheme \(S\) (*), and \(A=\underline{\mathcal O}_S\).

– \(\underline S\) is the topos of sheaves of sets on a complex analytic space \(S\), \(A=\underline{\mathcal O}_S\).

On the other hand, the sheaf of continuous complex functions on a topological space is in general not coherent.

Of course, if \((\underline S,A)\) is defined by a ringed space, the notion of coherence considered here coincides with the usual notion of Serre (EGA \(0_{\mathrm I}\) 5.3.1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7.

a) Let \(f:(\underline S',A')\longrightarrow(\underline S,A)\) be a morphism of ringed topoi. We assume \(f\) right flat, i.e. that the functor \(f^*=A'\otimes_A\,.\) is exact. There exists, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(_A\underline S)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(_A\underline S)\), a canonical functorial arrow:

\[f^*\,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\longrightarrow\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*(F),f^*(G))\;.\]

This arrow is an isomorphism when \(F\) is pseudo-coherent.

b) Suppose \(A\) commutative and coherent, and let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline S)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\). If \(\underline H^i(F)\) and \(\underline H^i(G)\) are coherent for every \(i\), the same holds for \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(F,G)\).
Proof. a) To define the arrow (cf. SGA 4 XVII), one may assume \(G\) has injective components, and one takes the composite arrow
\[f^*\,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*F,f^*G)\longrightarrow\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*F,f^*G)\;.\]

Let us show that it is an isomorphism for \(F\) pseudo-coherent. Denote for short by \(T\) (resp. \(T'\)) the functor \(f^*\,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(.,G)\) (resp. \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*.,f^*G)\)), and let \(a\in\mathbb Z\) be such that \(H^i(G)=0\) for \(i<a\). It is clear that we have \(TA\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}T'A\), hence, by dévissage, \(TF\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}T'F\) for \(F\) strictly perfect (2.1). On the other hand, if \(F\) is acyclic in degrees \(>n\), \(TF\) and \(T'F\) are acyclic in degrees \(<a-n\). To check that \(TF\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}T'F\) for \(F\) pseudo-coherent, one may, the question being local, assume \(F\) strictly \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, i.e. that we have a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &F''\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ F'\ar[rr]&&F\ar[ul] }\;,\]
with \(F'\) strictly perfect and \(F''\) acyclic in degrees \(\geq n-1\). One deduces from this a morphism of distinguished triangles
\[\xymatrix{ &TF''\ar[dl]|{-1}&\\ TF'&&TF\ar[ll]\ar[ul] }\qquad\longrightarrow\qquad\xymatrix{ &T'F''\ar[dl]|{-1}&\\ T'F'&&T'F\ar[ll]\ar[ul] }\;.\]

Now \(TF'\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}T'F'\) since \(F'\) is strictly perfect, and \(TF''\) and \(T'F''\) are acyclic in degrees \(\leq a-n\); writing the induced morphism on the exact cohomology sequences of the preceding triangles, one deduces that \(TF\longrightarrow T'F\) induces an isomorphism \(H^i(TF)\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}H^i(T'F)\) for \(i\leq a-n\). Since one can choose \(n\) arbitrarily large negative, we are done.
b) The proof is left as an exercise to the reader: see for example ([H] II 3.3).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.7.1.

We shall see below (7.1.2) another case of "compatibility" of \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) with \(\mathbf Lf^*\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7.2.

(cf. EGA \(0_{\mathrm I}\) 5.2.7). Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(s\) a point of \(\underline S\) (SGAA VIII 7.8), \(E\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\). If \(E_s\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}F_s\), there exists a \(s\)-pointed object \(U\) of \(\underline S\) such that \(E|U\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}F|U\).
Proof. Let \(\alpha:E_s\longrightarrow F_s\) and \(\beta:F_s\longrightarrow E_s\) be mutually inverse isomorphisms. By (2.22 a)), we have

\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)_s\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\operatorname{Hom}(E_s,F_s)\qquad(\text{resp. }\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E)_s\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\operatorname{Hom}(F_s,E_s)\,)\;.\]

There thus exist a "neighbourhood" \(U\) of \(s\) and a section \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) of \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E)\)) above \(U\) such that \(u_s=\alpha\) (resp. \(v_s=\beta\)). The homomorphisms \(vu:E|U\longrightarrow E|U\) and \(uv:F|U\longrightarrow F|U\) induce the identity at \(s\), hence on a suitable \(V\) above \(U\), qed.

footnotes.html: We introduce this notion here as a technical intermediary; the only notion to keep in mind is that of coherence.
In accordance with the new conventions, we shall say "scheme" for "prescheme", and "separated scheme" for "scheme".

blocks.html: 4. Perfect complexes

4.0. In this section, \(\underline S\) denotes a site, \(S\) an object of \(\underline S\), \(\underline C\) a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category and \(\underline C_0\) a strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) (1.1). Unless otherwise stated, we assume \(\underline C_0\) locally liftable in \(\underline C\) and locally stable under kernels of epimorphisms (1.2).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1

(*). Let \(u:E\to F\) be an arrow of \(C(\underline C_S)\). If \(E\) is strictly \(\underline C_0\)-perfect (2.1) and \(F\) is acyclic, then \(u\) is locally homotopic to zero.

Proof. The point is to construct locally a homotopy \(k\) such that \(u=dk+kd\). Since \(E\) is strictly perfect, there exist \(a,b\in\mathbb Z\), with \(a\leq b\), such that \(E^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\). One can therefore take \(k^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\). One then constructs \(k^i\) for \(i\geq a\) by descending induction on \(i\). Suppose constructed \(k^i:E^i\longrightarrow F^{i-1}\), in such a way that \(u^i=k^{i+1}d_E^i+d_F^{i-1}k^i\) for \(i\geq n>a\), and let us show that, after localizing if necessary, there exists \(k^{n-1}:E^{n-1}\longrightarrow F^{n-2}\) such that \(u^{n-1}=k^nd_E^{n-1}+d_F^{n-2}k^{n-1}\). By the induction hypothesis, the arrow
\[v^{n-1}=u^{n-1}-k^nd_E^{n-1}\;:\;E^{n-1}\longrightarrow F^{n-1}\]

factors through \(Z^{n-1}(F)\). But \(Z^{n-1}(F)=B^{n-1}(F)\) since \(F\) is acyclic. Since \(\underline C_0\) is locally liftable in \(\underline C\), it follows that, after localizing if necessary, there exists \(k^{n-1}:E^{n-1}\longrightarrow F^{n-2}\) such that \(v^{n-1}=d_F^{n-2}k^{n-1}\), qed.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.2.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ &P\ar[d]^v\\ E\ar[r]_f&F }\]

be a diagram of \(C(\underline C_S)\), where \(P\) is strictly \(\underline C_0\)-perfect and \(f\) a quasi-isomorphism. The set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists an arrow \(u:P_X\longrightarrow E_X\) of \(C(\underline C_X)\) such that the diagram

\[\xymatrix{ &P_X\ar[dl]_u\ar[d]^{v_X}\\ E_X\ar[r]_{f_X}&F_X }\]

is commutative in \(K(\underline C_X)\) is a refinement of \(S\).
Proof. Let \(G\) be the cone of \(f\). We have the exact sequence
\[\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{K(\underline C_S)}(P,E)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{K(\underline C_S)}(P,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{K(\underline C_S)}(P,G)\longrightarrow\;.\]

Since \(G\) is acyclic, the image of \(v\) in \(\operatorname{Hom}_{K(\underline C_S)}(P,G)\) is locally zero by (4.1). Hence, after localizing if necessary, there exists \(u:P\to E\) such that \(v=fu\) in \(K(\underline C_S)\), qed.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.

Let \(f:E\longrightarrow F\) be a quasi-isomorphism of \(C(\underline C_S)\), with \(F\) strictly perfect (relatively to \(\underline C_0\)). The set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(g:F_X\to E_X\) such that \(f_Xg\) is homotopic to \(\operatorname{Id}_{F_X}\) is a refinement of \(S\).
Proof. This is a particular case of (4.2).

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 4.4.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}K(\underline C_S)\). One can define (for example with the aid of a cleavage of \(\underline C\) (SGA 1 VI 7.1)) a category \((\operatorname{Qis}/F)\) fibered over \(\underline S_{/S}\), whose fibre at \(X\) above \(S\) is the category \((\operatorname{Qis}/F_X)^o\) (opposite of the category of quasi-isomorphisms with target \(F_X\)). One can then express (4.3) by saying that, if \(F\) is strictly perfect, the arrow \(\operatorname{Id}_F\) is locally cofinal in \((\operatorname{Qis}/F)\).

blocks.title: Notation
blocks.html:
Notation 4.5.

Let \(\underline M\) be a fibered \(\underline S\)-category. Let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and \(E\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline M_X\).
