We shall denote by \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\underline M}(E,F)\) the sheaf on \(\underline S_{/X}\) associated with the presheaf of \(\underline S\)-morphisms from \(E\) to \(F\) in \(\underline M\) (for the definition of this presheaf, see [G] I 2.6; one should beware that, even if \(\underline C\) is a stack ([G]), the corresponding presheaf for \(\underline M=K(\underline C)\) (or \(D(\underline C)\)) is not in general a sheaf).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.

Let \(E\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}C(\underline C_S)\), with \(E\) strictly perfect. The canonical arrow

\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{K(\underline C)}(E,F)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{D(\underline C)}(E,F)\]

is an isomorphism.
Proof. This is an immediate consequence of (4.4) and of the formula
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{D(\underline C_X)}(E_X,F_X)=\varinjlim_{(\operatorname{Qis}/E_X)^o}\operatorname{Hom}_{K(\underline C_X)}(\,\cdot\,,F_X)\]
for \(X\) above \(S\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.7.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\) and let \(a,b\in\mathbb Z\) be such that \(a\leq b\). We shall say that \(F\) is of \(\underline C_0\)-perfect amplitude contained in the interval \([a,b]\), and we shall write

\[\underline C_0\text{-parf-amp}(F)\subset[a,b]\;,\]

if the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists a quasi-isomorphism \(F'\longrightarrow F\) of \(C(\underline C_X)\) with \(F'\) strictly \(\underline C_0\)-perfect (2.1) and such that \(F'^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\), is a refinement of \(S\). (By virtue of (4.3) this condition is equivalent to the one obtained by replacing the expression "quasi-isomorphism of \(C(\underline C_X)\)" by "isomorphism of \(D(\underline C_X)\)".) We shall say that \(F\) is of finite \(\underline C_0\)-perfect amplitude if there exist integers \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(\underline C_0\text{-parf-amp}(F)\subset[a,b]\). We shall say that \(F\) is \(\underline C_0\)-perfect if \(F\) is locally of finite \(\underline C_0\)-perfect amplitude (*).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 4.8.

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(\underline C\) the \(\underline S\)-category defined by \(U\longmapsto\) (category of left \(A_U\)-Modules), \(\underline C_0\) the sub-\(\underline S\)-category defined by \(U\longmapsto\) (category of left \(A_U\)-Modules which are direct factors of free Modules of finite type). The pair \((\underline C,\underline C_0)\) satisfies, by (1.3.1), the hypotheses (4.0). An object \(F\) of \(D(_A\underline S)\) (cf.(2.15)) will be said to be of perfect amplitude contained in \([a,b]\) (resp. of finite perfect amplitude, resp. perfect) if \(F\) is of \(\underline C_0\)-perfect amplitude contained in \([a,b]\) (resp....). When the direct factors of free Modules of finite type are locally free (cf.(2.15.1)), to say that \(F\) is perfect thus means that \(F\) is locally isomorphic (in \(D(\underline S)\)) to a complex with bounded degrees and with components free of finite type.

blocks.title: Notation
blocks.html:
Notation 4.9.

We shall denote by \(D(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-parf}}\) or \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) the strictly full sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(D(\underline C)\) formed by the \(\underline C_0\)-perfect objects. In the case of example (4.8), we shall write \(D(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), or \(\operatorname{Parf}(_A\underline S)\), or even \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S)\) if there is no possible ambiguity about \(A\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.10

The category \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) is a triangulated sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(D(\underline C)\) (1.1.4). The canonical functor

\[K^b(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\]

(resp. \(\operatorname{Tr}(K^b(\underline C_0))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tr}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0))\) (1.1.3))

induces a \(\underline S\)-equivalence on the associated stacks ([G] II 2.2.4), which means:

(i) for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}S\) and all \(E,F\in\operatorname{ob}K^b(\underline C_{0X})\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Tr}(K^b(\underline C_{0X}))\)), the canonical arrow

\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{K^b(\underline C_0)}(E,F)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{D(\underline C)}(E,F)\]
\[(\text{resp.}\qquad\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\operatorname{Tr}(K^b(\underline C_0))}(E,F)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\operatorname{Tr}(D(\underline C))}(E,F)\;)\]

is an isomorphism, and

(ii) every object of \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Tr}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0))\)) is locally isomorphic in \(D(\underline C)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Tr}(D(\underline C))\)) to an object of \(K^b(\underline C_0)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Tr}(K^b(\underline C_0))\)).

Proof. The non-resp. assertion (i) is a particular case of (4.6) and the non-resp. assertion (ii) is true by definition ((4.7) and (4.9)). It is clear on the other hand that \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) is stable under translation of degrees. To prove that \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) is a triangulated sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(D(\underline C)\), it therefore suffices to show that if
\[(*)\qquad\xymatrix{ &G\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ E\ar[rr]^u&&F\ar[ul] }\]

is a distinguished triangle of \(D(\underline C)\) such that \(E\) and \(F\) are perfect, then \(G\) is perfect. After localizing if necessary, one may assume that \(E\) and \(F\) are objects of \(K^b(\underline C_0)\). After localizing further, one may assume, by the non-resp. assertion (i), that \(u\) is an arrow of \(K^b(\underline C_0)\); \(G\) is then perfect, being isomorphic to the cone of \(u\), which is strictly perfect. Hence \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) is indeed a triangulated sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(D(\underline C)\). The preceding argument also proves, moreover, the resp. assertion (ii). It remains to prove the resp. assertion (i). So let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and let \(E=(E_1\xrightarrow{\;a_1\;}E_2\xrightarrow{\;a_2\;}E_3\xrightarrow[{+1}]{\;a_3\;}E_1)\), \(F=(F_1\xrightarrow{\;b_1\;}F_2\xrightarrow{\;b_2\;}F_3\xrightarrow[{+1}]{\;b_3\;}F_1)\) be distinguished triangles of \(K^b(\underline C_{0X})\); let us show that the arrow
\[(**)\qquad\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\operatorname{Tr}(K^b(\underline C_0))}(E,F)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\operatorname{Tr}(D(\underline C))}(E,F)\]

is an isomorphism. By the non-resp. assertion (i), we already know that \((**)\) is injective. Let \((u_i):E\to F\) be a morphism of triangles in \(D(\underline C)\). After localizing if necessary, one may assume, by the non-resp. assertion (i), that \(u_i\) (\(1\leq i\leq3\)) is an arrow of \(K^b(\underline C_0)\). The arrow \(u_{i+1}a_i-b_iu_i\), for \(1\leq i\leq3\) (with the convention \(u_4=u_1\)), is zero in \(D(\underline C)\), hence locally zero in \(K^b(\underline C_0)\) by the non-resp. assertion (i). In other words, \((u_i)\) locally defines a morphism of triangles in \(K^b(\underline C_0)\). This proves that \((**)\) is surjective and completes the proof of (4.10).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.10.1.

One may replace, in (4.10), \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) by \(D^*(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-parf}}=D^*(\underline C)\cap\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) (\(*=-\) or \(b\)); there is nothing to change in the proof.

blocks.title: Complement
blocks.html:
Complement 4.11.

One can make the triangulated structure of \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) more precise in terms of perfect amplitude (4.7). If \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C)\) is of perfect amplitude contained in \([a,b]\), \(E[n]\) is of perfect amplitude contained in \([a-n,b-n]\). Let

\[\xymatrix{ &G\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ E\ar[rr]&&F\ar[ul] }\]

be a distinguished triangle of \(D(\underline C)\). One can draw up a table analogous to (2.6), where the entries indicate perfect amplitudes and each row corresponds to an implication whose target is underlined.

\[\begin{array}{ccc} E&F&G\\ {[a+1,b+1]}&{[a,b]}&\underline{[a,b]}\\ {[a,b]}&\underline{[a,b]}&{[a,b]}\\ \underline{[a,b]}&{[a,b]}&{[a-1,b-1]} \end{array}\;.\]
From (4.10) one deduces at once the following

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.12.

Suppose that \(\underline S\) is a punctual site. For \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C)\) to be of perfect amplitude (rel. to \(\underline C_0\)) contained in \([a,b]\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a quasi-isomorphism \(F'\longrightarrow F\), with \(F'\) strictly perfect and such that \(F'^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\). The canonical functors

\[K^b(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\]
\[\text{et}\qquad\operatorname{Tr}(K^b(\underline C_0))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Tr}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0))\]

are equivalences of categories.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.12.1.

When \(\underline S\) is a punctual site, the objects of \(\underline C_0\) are projective. When \(\underline C_0\) contains all the projective objects of \(\underline C\), one says "projective amplitude" instead of "\(\underline C_0\)-perfect amplitude".

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.12.2.

Let \(\underline C\) be an abelian category (which can be regarded as fibered over a punctual site), and let \(\underline C_0\) be a strictly full additive subcategory. Suppose \(\underline C_0\) stable under kernels of epimorphisms and quasi-liftable in \(\underline C\). Then an acyclic object of \(K^b(\underline C_0)\) is not in general zero, so there is no hope that the functor \(K^b(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow D(\underline C)\) is faithful. However, if \(K^{b,\varnothing}(\underline C_0)\) denotes the thick subcategory of \(K^b(\underline C_0)\) formed by the objects acyclic (in \(\underline C\)), the canonical functor

\[K^b(\underline C_0)/K^{b,\varnothing}(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow D(\underline C)\]

is fully faithful.

Hints: We already know (2.7) that the functor

\[K^-(\underline C_0)/K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow D^-(\underline C)\]

is fully faithful. Show, for example with the aid of the criterion ([V] 4.2.b) (ii)'), that the functor

\[K^b(\underline C_0)/K^{b,\varnothing}(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow K^-(\underline C_0)/K^{-,\varnothing}(\underline C_0)\]

is fully faithful.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.13.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\), \(a\), \(b\), \(c\in\mathbb Z\) be such that \(a\leq b\) and \(a\leq c\). If \(F\) is of perfect amplitude contained in \([a,c]\) (4.7) and if \(H^i(F)=0\) for \(i>b\), then \(F\) is of perfect amplitude contained in \([a,d]\), where \(d=\inf(b,c)\).

Proof. One may assume \(b\leq c\), and \(F\) strictly perfect such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,c]\). Then \(F\) is isomorphic to its truncation \(F'\) defined by \(F'^i=F^i\) for \(i<b\), \(F'^b=Z^b(F)\), \(F'^i=0\) for \(i>b\). But \(Z^b(F)\) admits a finite right resolution by objects of \(\underline C_0\), hence is locally an object of \(\underline C_0\), whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.14.

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_S\) and \(n\in\mathbb N\). Equivalent conditions:

(i) \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(F)\subset[-n,0]\);

(ii) \(F\) admits locally a left resolution of length \(n\) by objects of \(\underline C_0\), i.e. the set of the \(X\) above \(S\) such that there exists an exact sequence

\[0\longrightarrow L^{-n}\longrightarrow L^{-n+1}\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow L^0\longrightarrow F_X\longrightarrow0\;,\]

with \(L^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{0X}\) for every \(i\), is a refinement of \(S\).
Proof. This is an immediate consequence of (4.7) and (4.13).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.14.1.

We shall say that \(F\) is of perfect dimension \(\leq n\), and we shall write \(\mathrm{parf.dim}(F)\leq n\), if \(F\) satisfies the equivalent conditions of (4.14). We shall call the perfect dimension of \(F\) the smallest integer \(n\) such that \(\mathrm{parf.dim}(F)\leq n\) (if no such integer exists, we shall say that \(F\) is of infinite perfect dimension).

When \(\underline S\) is the punctual site and \(\underline C_0\) is the full subcategory of \(\underline C\) formed by the projective objects, the perfect dimension is none other than the usual projective dimension.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.15.

Let \(a\), \(b\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\) (\(a\leq b\), \(n>0\)), and let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C)\) be such that \(F^i=0\) (resp. \(\underline H^i(F)=0\)) for \(i\notin[a,b]\). If, for every \(i\in[a,b]\), \(F^i\) (resp. \(\underline H^i(F)\)) is of perfect dimension \(\leq n+(i-a)\), then \(F\) is of perfect amplitude contained in \([a-n,b]\). Consequently, if for every \(i\in[a,b]\), \(F^i\) (resp. \(\underline H^i(F)\)) is perfect, so is \(F\).
Proof. Proceed by induction on \(b\) (\(a\) and \(n\) fixed), truncating \(F\) and applying (4.11) (cf. proof of (2.11)).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.16.

Let \(a\), \(b\in\mathbb Z\) (\(a\leq b\)), and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C)\) be such that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\). We assume that we have \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(F)\subset[a,b]\), and that \(F^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\) for \(i>a\). Then \(F^a\) is locally an object of \(\underline C_0\).
Proof. We have an exact sequence (obtained by truncating \(F\))
\[0\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F^a[-a]\longrightarrow0\;,\]
with \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(F')\subset[a+1,b+1]\). From (4.11) and (4.13) one deduces that \(\mathrm{parf.dim}(F^a)=0\), i.e. that \(F^a\) is locally an object of \(\underline C_0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.17.

Let \(F'\), \(F''\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline C_S)\), and \(a\), \(b\in\mathbb Z\), with \(a\leq b\). Consider the conditions:

(i) \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(F')\subset[a,b]\) and \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(F'')\subset[a,b]\);

(ii) \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(F'+F'')\subset[a,b]\).

We have (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii), and, if \(\underline C_0\) is locally stable under direct factors (i.e. the relation \(E'+E''\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\), for \(E'\), \(E''\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_X\) and \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), implies that locally \(E'\) and \(E''\) are objects of \(\underline C_0\)), then we have (i)\(\Longleftrightarrow\)(ii).

Proof. The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) is a particular case of (4.11). Let us prove the converse. Set \(F=F'+F''\). Since \(F\) is pseudo-coherent, \(F'\) and \(F''\) are too (2.12). On the other hand, the relation \(H^i(F)=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\) implies \(H^i(F')=H^i(F'')=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\). By (2.10 a)), one may therefore assume (after localizing if necessary) that \(F'^i\) (resp. \(F''^i\)) \(\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\) for \(i\geq a+1\) and \(F'^i=F''^i=0\) for \(i>b\), and then, by truncating, that \(F'^i=F''^i=0\) for \(i<a\). Since we have \(\mathrm{parf.dim}(F)\subset[a,b]\), one deduces from (4.16) that \(F^a\) is locally an object of \(\underline C_0\). Now \(F^a=F'^a+F''^a\), hence \(F'^a\) and \(F''^a\) are locally objects of \(\underline C_0\) since \(\underline C_0\) is locally stable under direct factors.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.18.

Let \((\underline S',\underline C',\underline C'_0)\) be a trio satisfying the same hypotheses (4.0) as \((\underline S,\underline C,\underline C_0)\). Let \(f:\underline S'\longrightarrow\underline S\) be a morphism of sites (defined by \(f^{-1}:\underline S\longrightarrow\underline S'\)), and let \(u:D^*(\underline C)\longrightarrow D^*(\underline C')\) (\(*=-,+,\varnothing\)) be a functor above \(f\) (cf. (2.13)). If \(u\) takes the objects of \(\underline C_0\) to \(\underline C'_0\)-perfect complexes, then \(u\) defines a functor \(D^*(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-parf}}\longrightarrow D^*(\underline C')_{\underline C'_0\text{-parf}}\).

Proof. Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-parf}}\); let us show that \(u(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C')_{\underline C'_0\text{-parf}}\). The question being local, one may assume \(F\) strictly perfect. It only remains to dévisser, taking into account the fact that \(D^*(\underline C')_{\underline C'_0\text{-parf}}\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(D^*(\underline C')\) (4.10).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.18.1.

Suppose that there exist \(m\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\) (\(m\leq n\)) such that \(F\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\) implies \(\underline C'_0\text{-parf.amp}(u(F))\subset[m,n]\). Then, if \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^*(\underline C)\), we have

\[\underline C_0\text{-parf.amp}(F)\subset[a,b]\Longrightarrow\underline C'_0\text{-parf.amp}(u(F))\subset[a+m,b+n]\;.\]

The proof is left as an exercise to the reader \(utiliser (4.11)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.19.

Under the hypotheses of (2.16), if \(E'\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_{A'}\underline S'_A)\) is perfect as an object of \(D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\) and \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_A\underline S)\) is perfect, then \(E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}E\) is perfect.

Proof. One may assume that \(E'\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_{A'}S'_A)\) (\(S'\) = final object of \(\underline S'\)), and one applies (4.18) to \(u=E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}:D^-(_A\underline S)\longrightarrow D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\).
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.19.1.

a) The functor \(\mathbf Lf^*:D^-(_A\underline S)\longrightarrow D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\) induces a functor

\[\mathbf Lf^*:D^-(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D^-(_{A'}\underline S')_{\mathrm{parf}}\;.\]

b) If \(A\) is commutative, the functor \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A:D^-(\underline S)\times_{\underline S}D^-(\underline S)\longrightarrow D^-(\underline S)\) induces a functor

\[\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\;:\;D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\times_{\underline S}D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\;.\]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.19.2.

One can make (4.19) more precise in terms of perfect amplitude:

\[\mathrm{parf.amp}(E)\subset[a,b]\text{ et }\mathrm{parf.amp}(E')\subset[a',b']\Longrightarrow\mathrm{parf.amp}(E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}E)\subset[a+a',b+b']\;.\]

In particular:

\[\mathrm{parf.amp}(E)\subset[a,b]\Longrightarrow\mathrm{parf.amp}(\mathbf Lf^*(E))\subset[a,b]\;.\]

footnotes.html: This lemma was communicated to the writer by D. Ferrand.
Of course, when this cannot lead to confusion, we shall omit \(\underline C_0\) in the preceding expressions.

blocks.html: 5. Finite tor-dimension and perfection

5.0. The reader will surely have asked himself the question: what does a pseudo-coherent complex \(E\) lack in order to be perfect? Does it suffice, for example, that \(E\) have bounded cohomology? The answer is no. Indeed, take for \(\underline C\) the category of modules over a commutative noetherian ring \(A\), and for \(\underline C_0\) the full subcategory of projective \(A\)-modules of finite type. Any \(A\)-module of finite type \(E\) is pseudo-coherent and with bounded cohomology, but \(E\) is perfect only if \(E\) is in addition of finite projective dimension (or, what amounts to the same, of finite tor-dimension). This example suggests that the difference between a pseudo-coherent complex and a perfect complex lies in a question of cohomological dimension. This is what we shall now examine in the case of the category of Modules of a ringed topos (cf.(3.3)). We shall need a few preliminary sorites on the notion of finite tor-dimension. We shall be brief, since this question already appears in the literature: cf. ([H] II 4) and (SGA 4 XVII 4.1.9.).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, and \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_AX)\), \(m\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\) such that \(m\leq n\). Equivalent conditions:

(i) \(F\) is isomorphic, in \(D^-(_AX)\), to a complex \(F'\) such that \(F'^i\) is flat for every \(i\) and zero for \(i\notin[m,n]\);

(ii) for every right \(A_X\)-Module \(E\), we have \(\underline H^i(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}F)=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\).
Proof. The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) is trivial; the implication (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) follows, by truncation, from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.1.1.

(cf. (4.16)). Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_AX)\). We suppose that \(F\) satisfies hypothesis (ii) of (5.1), that \(F^i=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\), and that \(F^i\) is flat for \(i\neq m\). Then \(F^m\) is flat.
Proof. We have a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &F^m[-m]\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ F'\ar[rr]&&F\ar[ul] }\;,\]

where \(F'\) has components which are flat and zero outside the interval \([m+1,n]\) (assume \(m<n\)). Apply the functor \(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}\,.\) for \(E\) an arbitrary right \(A_X\)-Module, and write out the exact cohomology sequence.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.2.

An object \(F\) of \(D^-(_A\underline S)\) satisfying the equivalent conditions of (5.1) will be said to be of flat amplitude contained in the interval \([m,n]\), and we shall write

\[\mathrm{tor.amp}(F)\subset[m,n]\;.\]

We shall say that \(F\) is of finite flat amplitude (or of finite tor-dimension) if there exist integers \(m\) and \(n\) such that the preceding condition is satisfied.

5.3. Let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\). We shall denote by \(D^-(_AX)_{\mathrm{torf}}\) the full subcategory of \(D^-(_AX)\) formed of the objects of finite tor-dimension. By criterion (ii) of (5.1), it is a triangulated subcategory of \(D^-(_AX)\). For variable \(X\), the categories \(D^-(_AX)_{\mathrm{torf}}\) form a triangulated sub-\(\underline S\)-category (1.1.4) of \(D^-(_A\underline S)\), which we shall denote by \(D^-(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{torf}}\). The functor \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\) induces an exact \(\underline S\)-functor
\[\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\;:\;D^b(\underline S_A)\times_{\underline S}D(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{torf}}\longrightarrow D^b(\underline S_{\mathbb Z})\;.\]

5.4. For a left \(A\)-Module \(F\) to be of flat amplitude contained in \([-n,0]\) (\(n\in\mathbb N\)) it is necessary and sufficient that \(F\) be of tor-dimension \(\leq n\) in the ordinary sense, i.e. that \(F\) admit a left resolution of length \(n\) by flat \(A\)-Modules.

5.5. Let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and \(m\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\) (\(m\leq n\)). For \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_AX)\) to be of flat amplitude contained in \([m,n]\) it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a covering family \((X_i\longrightarrow X)\) such that \(F\,|\,X_i\) is of flat amplitude contained in \([m,n]\). In particular, if \(\underline S_{/X}\) is of finite type (SGA 4 VI 1.7), and if \(F\) is locally of finite tor-dimension, then \(F\) is of finite tor-dimension.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.6.

Under the hypotheses of (2.16), if \(E'\) is of flat amplitude contained in \([m',n']\) as an object of \(D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\) and if \(E\) is of flat amplitude contained in \([m,n]\), then \(E'\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}E\) is of flat amplitude contained in \([m+m',n+n']\).

Proof. This is immediate: use the form (5.1 (i)) of the notion of finite tor-dimension and the fact that, if \(E\) is a flat left \(A\)-Module, then \(f^{-1}(E)\) is a flat left \(f^{-1}(A)\)-Module (SGA 4 IV App. II).
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6.1.

a) The functor \(\mathbf Lf^*:D^-(_A\underline S)\longrightarrow D^-(_{A'}\underline S')\) induces a functor

\[\mathbf Lf^*:D(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{torf}}\longrightarrow D(_{A'}\underline S')_{\mathrm{torf}}\;.\]

b) If \(A\) is commutative, the functor \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A:D^-(\underline S)\times_{\underline S}D^-(\underline S)\longrightarrow D^-(\underline S)\) induces a functor

\[\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\;:\;D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{torf}}\times_{\underline S}D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{torf}}\longrightarrow D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{torf}}\;.\]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.6.2.

Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_A\underline S)\) (\(S\) = final object of \(\underline S\)), and \(m\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\) (\(m\leq n\)). If \(E\) is of flat amplitude contained in \([m,n]\), the same is true, by (5.6), of \(E_s\) for every point \(s\) of \(\underline S\). The converse is true if \(\underline S\) has enough points (use the form (5.1.(ii)) of the notion of flat amplitude, and the fact that \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\) commutes with passage to stalks).

blocks.title: Exercises
blocks.html:
Exercises 5.7.

a) Suppose \(A\) commutative and \((\underline S',A')\) faithfully flat over \((\underline S,A)\) (cf.(2.16.2)). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(_A\underline S)\) (1) to be of flat amplitude contained in \([m,n]\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f^*(E)\) be so.

b) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(_A\underline S)\) (1). Show the equivalence of the conditions:

(i) \(F\) is isomorphic, in \(D^+(_A\underline S)\), to a complex \(F'\) such that \(F'^i\) is injective for every \(i\) and zero for \(i\notin[m,n]\);

(ii) for every left \(A_S\)-Module \(E\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(E,F)=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\).

If \(F\) satisfies the preceding conditions, we say that \(F\) is of injective amplitude contained in \([m,n]\). The full subcategory of \(D^+(_A\underline S)\) formed of the objects of finite injective amplitude is a triangulated subcategory, denoted \(D^+(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{injf}}\). Show that, if \(A\) is commutative, the functor \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}:D(\underline S)^o\times D^+(\underline S)\longrightarrow D(\underline S)\) induces a functor

\[\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\;:\;D(\underline S)^o_{\mathrm{torf}}\times D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{injf}}\longrightarrow D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{injf}}\;.\]
We can now answer the question of (5.0) and state the main result of this section.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.8.

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, and \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(_A\underline S)\), \(m\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\), with \(m\leq n\). Equivalent conditions:

(i) \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(E)\subset[m,n]\) (4.8);

(ii) \(E\) is \((m-1)\)-pseudo-coherent (2.15) and we have \(\mathrm{tor.amp}(E)\subset[m,n]\) (5.2).
Since "perfect" obviously implies "pseudo-coherent", we deduce:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.8.1.

For \(E\) to be perfect (4.8), it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be pseudo-coherent (2.15) and locally of finite tor-dimension (5.2).
For the proof, we shall need a few lemmas.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.8.2.

Let \(B\) be a ring of \(\underline S\). If \(E\) is a left \(A\)-Module, \(G\) a right \(B\)-Module, and \(F\) an \(A\)-\(B\)-Bimodule (on the left over \(A\), on the right over \(B\)), there exists a canonical functorial homomorphism (5.8.2.1)

\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F,G)\otimes_AE\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F),G)\;.\]

The latter is an isomorphism for \(G\) injective and \(E\) of finite presentation.
Proof. Let us first define the arrow. By the adjunction formula dear to Cartan, it amounts to the same to define a homomorphism of \(B\)-Modules:
\[(*)\qquad\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F,G)\otimes_AE\otimes_{\mathbb Z_S}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\longrightarrow G\qquad(^1)\;.\]
Now we have a canonical homomorphism of \(A\)-\(B\)-biModules
\[(**)\qquad E\otimes_{\mathbb Z_S}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\longrightarrow F\]
(coming by adjunction from the identity arrow of \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\)). We likewise have a canonical homomorphism of \(B\)-Modules
\[(***)\qquad\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_B(F,G)\otimes_AF\longrightarrow G.\]

We then define \((*)\) as the composite of \((**)\) and \((***)\). To see that the arrow (5.8.2.1) is an isomorphism when \(G\) is injective and \(E\) of finite presentation, it suffices to remark that, if \(G\) is injective, the source and target of the arrow are right exact functors in \(E\) and that the arrow induces the identity for \(E=A\).
Here now is a particular case of (5.8).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.8.3.

Let \(E\) be a left \(A\)-Module. Equivalent conditions:

(i) \(E\) is locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type;

(ii) \(E\) is flat and of finite presentation.
Proof (following BOURBAKI Alg. Comm. chap. I, § 2 Exer 15).

It is clear that (i) implies (ii). Let us prove (ii)\(\Longrightarrow\)(i). By virtue of (1.3.1), we must show that the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,.)\) is exact. Let \(F\longrightarrow F''\longrightarrow0\) be an exact sequence of (left) \(A\)-Modules. We must show that the sequence
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F'')\longrightarrow0\]

is exact. For this it suffices to prove that, for every injective \(\mathbb Z_S\)-Module \(I\), the sequence deduced by applying the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathbb Z}(.,I)\) is exact. Now we have a commutative diagram, in which the vertical arrows are the canonical arrows of (3.17.1):

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ 0\ar[r]&{\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathbb Z_S}(F'',I)\otimes_AE}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathbb Z_S}(F,I)\otimes_AE}\ar[d]\\ 0\ar[r]&{\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathbb Z_S}(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F''),I)}\ar[r]&{\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathbb Z_S}(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_A(E,F),I)} }\;.\]
The vertical arrows are isomorphisms by virtue of (5.8.2). The top row is exact because \(E\) is flat. Hence the bottom row is exact, qed.

Proof of (5.8). The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) follows trivially from the definitions. Let us prove (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). The question being local, we may assume \(E\) strictly \((m-1)\)-pseudo-coherent. Since on the other hand \(E\) is acyclic in degree \(<m\), the truncation arrow
\[\xymatrix{ \ar[r]&E^{m-1}\ar[r]\ar[d]&E^m\ar[r]\ar[d]&E^{m+1}\ar[r]\ar[d]&\cdots\\ \ar[r]&0\ar[r]&E^m/B^m\ar[r]&E^{m+1}\ar[r]&\cdots }\]

is a quasi-isomorphism. Let us denote by \(E'\) the truncated complex. By construction, \(E'\) is of flat amplitude \(\subset[m,n]\) and \(E'^i\) is free of finite type for \(i>m\). Hence, by (5.1.1), \(E'^m\) is flat. On the other hand we have a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &E'^m[-m]\ar[dl]|{+1}&\\ E''\ar[rr]&&E'\ar[ul] }\;,\]

where \(E''\) is strictly perfect. By (2.6), \(E'^m[-m]\) is \((m-1)\)-pseudo-coherent, i.e. (2.9) \(E'^m\) is of finite presentation. We conclude thanks to (5.8.3) that \(E'^m\) is locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type, hence that we have \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(E)\subset[m,n]\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 5.8.4.

a) Using (2.5), we recover the fact that the perfect complexes form a triangulated subcategory of \(D(_A\underline S)\).

b) A perfect complex, even one with bounded cohomology, is not necessarily of finite tor-dimension. This is however the case if \(\underline S\) is of finite type (5.5). Let us denote by \(D(_A\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) the full sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(D^b(_A\underline S)\) formed of the complexes which are perfect and of finite tor-dimension. It follows at once from (2.16.1 b)) and (5.3) that, if \(A\) is commutative, the functor \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\) induces a functor

\[\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A\;:\;D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\times_{\underline S}D^b(\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^b(\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}\;.\]

This formula is the starting point of the theory of intersections on schemes. Note that on the other hand the (derived) tensor product of two pseudo-coherent complexes with bounded cohomology does not in general have bounded cohomology.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.9.

Suppose that \(\underline S=(\mathrm{Ens})\) (so that \(A\) "is" an ordinary ring) and that every left \(A\)-module is of finite tor-dimension (this is the case for example if \(A\) is of finite global cohomological dimension, see (MULT VI 2.6) and (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 17.2.8)). Then we have

(i) \(D^b(_A\underline S)=D(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{torf}}\), and

(ii) \(D^b(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{coh}}=D(_A\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\).

In other words, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(_A\underline S)\) to be of finite tor-dimension (resp. of finite tor-dimension and perfect) it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) have bounded cohomology (resp. have bounded cohomology and be pseudo-coherent).

Proof. By (5.8), it suffices to prove (i). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(_A\underline S)\). We may assume (after extending \(E\) by 0) that \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(_A\underline S)\), where \(S\) is the final object of \(\underline S\). Proceeding by induction on the number of nonzero cohomology objects of \(E\), we reduce, by dévissage, to the case where \(E\) is an ordinary \(A\)-Module, and we are done.
One can state more generally:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.10

Let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\). Suppose that \(\underline S_{/X}\) has enough points (SGA 4 IV 6.4.1.) and that, for every point \(s\) of \(\underline S_{/X}\), every left \(A_s\)-module is of finite tor-dimension. Then we have

\[D^b(_AX)_{\mathrm{coh}}=D^b(_AX)_{\mathrm{parf}}\;.\]

In other words, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(_AX)\) to be pseudo-coherent it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be perfect.

Proof. Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(_AX)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) and let \(s\) be a point of \(\underline S_{/X}\). We must show that \(E\) is perfect "in a neighborhood of \(s\)", i.e. that there exists a \(s\)-pointed object \(U\) of \(\underline S\) above \(X\) such that \(E\,|\,U\) is perfect. Now, the functor \(s^*\) being exact, we have, by (2.16.1 a)), \(E_s\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(A_s)_{\mathrm{coh}}\). Hence, by virtue of (5.9), \(E_s\) is perfect. The assertion will therefore be a consequence of (3.7.2) and of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.10.1.

Let \(s\) be a point of \(\underline S\) and let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(A_s)_{\mathrm{parf}}\). There exists a \(s\)-pointed object \(U\) of \(\underline S\) and \(F'\in\operatorname{ob}D(_{A_U})_{\mathrm{parf}}\) such that \(F'_s=F\).

Proof. This is obvious if \(F\) is a free \(A_s\)-module of finite type. Suppose now that \(F\) is projective of finite type, hence the image of a projector \(p:L\to L\) where \(L\) is free of finite type. There then exist a "neighborhood" \(U\) of \(s\), a free \(A_U\)-Module \(L'\) and a homomorphism \(p':L'\longrightarrow L'\) such that \(p'_s=p\). Since \(p'^2_s=p'_s\), we may assume, after shrinking \(U\), that \(p'^2=p'\). Hence \(F'=\operatorname{Im}(p')\) is a direct factor of \(L'\) and satisfies \(F'_s=F\). One generalizes immediately to the case where \(F\) is strictly perfect, i.e. a complex in bounded degrees composed of projective modules of finite type.

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 5.11.

Corollary (5.10) applies in particular to the case where \((\underline S,A)\) is defined by a space ringed in regular local rings, for example a regular scheme, or a complex analytic space smooth over \(\mathbb C\).

footnotes.html: we denote by \(S\) the final object of \(\underline S\) and by \(\mathbb Z_S\) the constant sheaf with value \(\mathbb Z\).

blocks.html: 6. Rank of a perfect complex

6.0. Let \((X,\underline{\mathcal O}_X)\) be a space ringed in local rings. It is well known that one can attach to every locally free sheaf of finite type \(L\) on an open \(U\) of \(X\) a function \(\mathrm{rg}_U(L)\) which is locally constant on \(U\) with integer values, called the rank of \(L\), the family of functions \(\mathrm{rg}_U(L)\) being characterized by the following properties:
(i) if \(V\subset U\) is an inclusion of opens, we have \(\mathrm{rg}_U(L\,|\,V)=\mathrm{rg}_U(L)\,|\,V\);
(ii) for every exact sequence of locally free \(\underline{\mathcal O}_U\)-Modules of finite type
\[0\longrightarrow L'\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow L''\longrightarrow0\;,\]
we have \(\mathrm{rg}_U(L)=\mathrm{rg}_U(L')+\mathrm{rg}_U(L'')\);
(iii) \(\mathrm{rg}_X(\underline{\mathcal O}_X)=1\).
Our aim, in this section, is to extend the notion of rank to perfect complexes.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1

(cf. SGA 5 VIII 2). Let \(C\) be an additive (resp. triangulated) category and \(F\) an abelian group. We say that a function \(f:\operatorname{ob}(C)\longrightarrow F\) is an additive function from \(C\) to \(F\) if for all \(L\), \(L'\), \(L''\in\operatorname{ob}(C)\) such that we have \(L\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}L'+L''\) (resp. a distinguished triangle

\[\xymatrix{ &L''\ar[dl]&\\ L'\ar[rr]&&L\ar[ul] }\qquad)\]

we have \(f(L)=f(L')+f(L'')\).

The additive functions from \(C\) to \(F\) form an abelian group, denoted \(\operatorname{Add}(C,F)\).

6.2. Let \(C\) be a triangulated category, \(f\) an additive function from \(C\) to an abelian group \(F\). Then \(f\) induces an additive function on the additive category \(C^{\mathrm{ad}}\) underlying \(C\), whence an inclusion \(\operatorname{Add}(C,F)\subset\operatorname{Add}(C^{\mathrm{ad}},F)\). In particular, we have \(f(0)=0\) and \(f(L)=f(M)\) if \(L\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}M\). On the other hand we have \(f(L[n])=(-1)^nf(L)\) for all \(L\in\operatorname{ob}(C)\) and \(n\in\mathbb Z\).

6.3. Let \(C\) be an additive (resp. triangulated) category. The group \(\operatorname{Add}(C,F)\) depends functorially on \(F\). The functor \(\operatorname{Add}(C,.)\) is representable (cf. SGA 5 VIII 2) by an abelian group denoted \(k(C)\) (*) equipped with an additive map \(\mathrm{cl}_0:\operatorname{ob}(C)\longrightarrow k(C)\) (if \(C\) is additive, we take for \(k(C)\) the group completion of the monoid of isomorphism classes of objects of \(C\); if \(C\) is triangulated, we take for \(k(C)\) the quotient of the free abelian group generated by \(\operatorname{ob}(C)\) by the relations identifying \(L\) with \(L'+L''\) whenever we have a distinguished triangle \(L'\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow L''\longrightarrow L'[1]\)).

The group \(k(C)\) depends functorially on \(C\) (with respect to additive (resp. exact) functors). If \(u\), \(u':C\to D\) are isomorphic additive (resp. exact) functors, then we have \(k(u)=k(u')\).
If \(C\) is a triangulated category, we have a canonical epimorphism \(k(C^{\mathrm{ad}})\longrightarrow k(C)\) (cf. (6.2)).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.4.

Let \(C\) be an additive category. The canonical functor \(C\to K^b(C)\) induces an isomorphism (functorial in \(C\)) \(k(C)\longrightarrow k(K^b(C))\).
Proof. This is an immediate consequence of the following formula (which follows trivially from (6.2)):
\[(6.4.1)\qquad f(L)=\sum(-1)^if(L^i)\]
for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}K^b(C)\) and \(f\) an additive function on \(K^b(C)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.5.

Let \(\underline S\) be a category, \(\underline C\) an additive (resp. triangulated) \(\underline S\)-category (1.1.1), \(F\) an abelian presheaf on \(\underline S\). We say that a family of functions \(f_X:\operatorname{ob}(\underline C_X)\longrightarrow F(X)\) indexed by \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) is an additive \(\underline S\)-function from \(\underline C\) to \(F\) if the following conditions are satisfied:

(i) for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_X\), \(f_X\) is an additive function (6.1);

(ii) for every arrow \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) of \(\underline S\), the following diagram is commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ \operatorname{ob}\underline C_Y\ar[r]^{f_Y}\ar[d]_{u^*}&F(Y)\ar[d]^{F(u)}\\ \operatorname{ob}\underline C_X\ar[r]_{f_X}&F(X) }\;.\]

The additive \(\underline S\)-functions from \(\underline C\) to \(F\) form an abelian group, denoted \(\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\underline C,F)\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 6.6.

a) Denote by \(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}}\) the fibered \(\underline S\)-category whose fiber at \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) is the category having the same objects as \(\underline C_X\) but whose arrows are the isomorphisms of \(\underline C_X\). Denote on the other hand by \(\underline F\) the fibered \(\underline S\)-category whose fiber at \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) is the discrete category (i.e. the only arrows are the identity arrows) \(F(X)\), the inverse image functor under \(u\in\mathrm{Fl}(\underline S)\) being \(F(u)\). Then an additive \(\underline S\)-function from \(\underline C\) to \(F\) is nothing other than a cartesian \(\underline S\)-functor from \(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}}\) to \(\underline F\) inducing on each fiber an additive function in the sense of (6.1). It will be convenient for us to interpret additivity in the following way. Suppose, to fix ideas, that \(\underline C\) is triangulated. The category \(\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)\) (resp. \(\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C)^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)\) (1.1.2)) of cartesian \(\underline S\)-functors from \(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}}\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C)^{\mathrm{is}}\)) to \(\underline F\) is the discrete category associated with an abelian group, and we have a homomorphism

\[\partial\;:\;\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C)^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)\;,\]

defined by \(\partial(f)(T)=f(L)-f(L')-f(L'')\) for \(f\in\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}},F)\) and \(T=(L'\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow L''\longrightarrow L'[1])\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C)\). To say that \(f\in\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)\) is additive means that \(\partial(f)=0\), in other words we have a (canonical functorial) exact sequence

\[(6.6.1)\quad0\to\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\underline C,F)\to\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\underline C^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)\xrightarrow{\;\partial\;}\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Tr}(\underline C)^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)\;.\]

b) Let \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) be an arrow of \(\underline S\). The "inverse image under \(u\)" functors, being all isomorphic to one another, define (6.3) a unique homomorphism \(k(Y)\longrightarrow k(X)\). Consequently, \(X\mapsto k(X)\) defines an abelian presheaf on \(\underline S\), denoted \(k(\underline C)\). An additive \(\underline S\)-function from \(\underline C\) to \(F\) is then interpreted simply as a homomorphism of presheaves \(k(\underline C)\longrightarrow F\), in other words we have

\[\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\underline C,F)=\operatorname{Hom}(k(\underline C),F)\;.\]

The presheaf \(k(\underline C)\) depends, of course, functorially on \(\underline C\) with respect to additive (resp. exact) \(\underline S\)-functors.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.7.

Let \(\underline S\) be a site, \(\underline C\) a flat abelian \(\underline S\)-category, \(\underline C_0\) a strictly full additive sub-\(\underline S\)-category of \(\underline C\) satisfying the hypotheses (4.0). Then the morphism of presheaves

\[k(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow k(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0))\qquad(\text{cf. }(4.9))\;,\]

induced by the inclusion of \(\underline C_0\) into \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\), defines an isomorphism on the associated sheaves. In other words, for every abelian sheaf \(F\) on \(\underline S\), the restriction homomorphism

\[\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0),F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\underline C_0,F)\]

is an isomorphism.
Proof. Let \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on \(\underline S\); let us show that the arrow
\[\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0),F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\underline C_0,F)\]
is an isomorphism. It factors as

\[\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0),F)\xrightarrow{\;a\;}\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(K^b(\underline C_0),F)\xrightarrow{\;b\;}\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\underline C_0,F)\;.\]
Now, by (6.4), the arrow \(b\) is an isomorphism. We are thus reduced to proving that \(a\) is an isomorphism.
Consider the following commutative diagram, where the rows are exact (6.6.1), and the vertical arrows are the "restriction" arrows:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.2em{ 0\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C),F)}\ar[r]\ar[d]_a&{\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C)^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Tr}(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C))^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)}\ar[d]\\ 0\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Add}_{\underline S}(K^b(\underline C_0),F)}\ar[r]&{\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(K^b(\underline C_0)^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)}\ar[r]&{\underline{\operatorname{Cart}}_{\underline S}(\operatorname{Tr}(K^b(\underline C_0))^{\mathrm{is}},\underline F)} }\;.\]
Since \(F\) is a sheaf, \(\underline F\) is a stack (G II 2.2.4), hence, by (4.10), the two right-hand vertical arrows are isomorphisms. The same therefore holds for \(a\), qed.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.7.1.

(cf.4.10.1). One can, in (6.7), replace \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline C,\underline C_0)\) by \(D^*(\underline C)_{\underline C_0\text{-parf}}\) (\(*=-\) or \(b\)).
6.8. Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(\underline C\) and \(\underline C_0\) the fibered \(\underline S\)-categories considered in (4.8). We have homomorphisms of abelian presheaves
\[k(\underline C_0)\xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;}k(D^-(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})\xrightarrow{\;\beta\;}k(\operatorname{Parf}(_A\underline S))\;,\]

which, by (6.7), induce isomorphisms on the associated sheaves. The homomorphism \(\alpha\) is compatible with inverse images, i.e. if \(u:(\underline S',A')\longrightarrow(\underline S,A)\) is a morphism of ringed topoi, we have a commutative diagram, where the vertical arrows are induced by the functor \(\mathbf Lu^*\) (cf. (4.19.1)):
\[\xymatrix@C=2em{ k(\underline C_0)\ar[r]\ar[d]&k(D^-(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})\ar[d]\\ k(\underline C'_0)\ar[r]&k(D^-(_{A'}\underline S')_{\mathrm{parf}}) }\]

(\(\underline C'_0\) being defined analogously to \(\underline C_0\)). If \(A\) is commutative, the tensor product \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}\) equips \(k(\underline C_0)\) and \(k(D^-(_A\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})\) with structures of presheaves of rings (cf. (4.19.1)), and \(\alpha\) is a homomorphism of presheaves of rings.

6.9. In the situation of (6.8), suppose that \((\underline S,A)\) is locally ringed (cf. (2.15.1)). Then (2.15.1) for every \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(\underline C_{0X}\) is the category of locally free \(A_X\)-Modules of finite type. Denote by \(\underline C_1\) the fibered \(\underline S\)-category defined by \(X\longmapsto\) (category of free \(A_X\)-Modules of finite type). It is clear that \(\underline C_1\) satisfies the hypotheses (4.0). An object of \(D(\underline S)\) is perfect if and only if it is perfect relative to \(\underline C_1\). We have homomorphisms of abelian presheaves
\[k(\underline C_1)\longrightarrow k(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow k(D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})\longrightarrow k(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S))\]

(the two on the left being homomorphisms of presheaves of rings), which induce, by (6.7), isomorphisms on the associated sheaves. We will show that the associated sheaves are in fact canonically isomorphic to the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.10.

Let \((\underline S,A)\) be a locally ringed topos, \(U\) an object of \(\underline S\) distinct from the empty object, \(p\), \(q\in\mathbb N\). If we have \(A_U^p\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}A_U^q\), then \(p=q\).

Proof. By an argument similar to the one given in the proof of (2.15.1), we reduce to the case where \((\underline S,A)\) is the locally ringed topos defined by a prescheme, and in that case the assertion is trivial (take a point in \(U\)).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.11.

We call rank, and denote by \(\mathrm{rg}\), the additive \(\underline S\)-function from \(\underline C_1\) to \(\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\) defined by

\[\mathrm{rg}(L)=p\text{ si }L\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}A_X^p\;,\;X\neq\varnothing\;,\]
\[\mathrm{rg}(L)=0\text{ si }L\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_{1X},\;X=\varnothing\;.\]

By virtue of (6.7), the rank function extends uniquely to an additive \(\underline S\)-function from \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S)\) to \(\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\) (which we shall still call rank and denote by \(\mathrm{rg}\)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.12.

a) For \(L\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (\(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\)), we have \(\mathrm{rg}(L\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)=\mathrm{rg}(L)\,\mathrm{rg}(M)\).

b) Let \(u:(\underline S',A')\longrightarrow(\underline S,A)\) be a morphism of locally ringed topoi. For \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), we have \(\mathrm{rg}(\mathbf Lu^*(M))=u^*(\mathrm{rg}(M))\).

In other words, the rank homomorphism: \(k(D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\) is a homomorphism of presheaves of rings, compatible with inverse images.

Proof. It suffices to show that the homomorphism induced by the rank on the sheaf associated with \(k(D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})\) is a homomorphism of sheaves of rings compatible with inverse images. By (6.9), we may replace \(k(D^-(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})\) by \(k(\underline C_1)\). We are thus reduced to verifying a) and b) for \(L\) and \(M\) free of finite type, which is trivial.

6.13. \((\underline S,A)\) still denoting a locally ringed topos, let \(X\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and \(\underline n\in H^0(X,\mathbb Z_{\underline S})\). We define an object of \(\operatorname{Parf}(X)\), denoted \(A_X^{\underline n}\), in the following way. If \(\underline n\) is constant with value an integer \(n\geq0\) (resp. \(n<0\)), we set \(A_X^{\underline n}=A_X^n\) (resp. \(A_X^{\underline n}=A_X^{-n}[1]\)); in the general case, we decompose \(X\) into disjoint pieces \(X_i\) such that \(\underline n|X_i\) is constant, and \(A_X^{\underline n}\) is defined as the unique object of \(\operatorname{Parf}(X)\) inducing \(A_{X_i}^{\underline n|X_i}\) on each \(X_i\). (One can, more generally, define \(L^{\underline n}\) for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Parf}(X)\), by setting \(L^{\underline n}=A_X^{\underline n}\otimes L\).).

The map \(i_X:H^0(X,\mathbb Z_{\underline S})\longrightarrow k(\operatorname{Parf}(X))\), defined by \(i_X(\underline n)=\mathrm{cl}(A_X^{\underline n})\), is a ring homomorphism (such that \(i_X(1)=1\)), and defines, for varying \(X\), a homomorphism of presheaves of rings
\[i:\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\longrightarrow k(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S))\;.\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.14.

Under the hypotheses of (6.13), we have

\[\mathrm{rg}\,i=\operatorname{Id}(\mathbb Z_{\underline S})\]

(in other words, \(k(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S))\), considered as a presheaf of \(\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\)-algebras by means of \(i\), is augmented toward \(\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\) by the rank). Moreover the rank

\[\mathrm{rg}:k(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S))\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\]

defines an isomorphism on the associated sheaves.

Proof. The first assertion follows trivially from the relations \(\mathrm{cl}(L[1])=-\,\mathrm{cl}(L)\), \(\mathrm{cl}(L^n)=n\,\mathrm{cl}(L)\) for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S)\). Let us prove the second. We first remark that \(i\) factors in an obvious way through \(k(\underline C_0)\). Since \(\mathrm{rg}\,i\) is the identity, we are reduced (by (6.9)) to proving that the homomorphism \(i\,\mathrm{rg}:k(\underline C_0)\longrightarrow k(\underline C_0)\) induces an isomorphism on the associated sheaves, i.e. that, for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_0\), \(i\,\mathrm{rg}(L)\) is locally isomorphic to \(L\), which is immediate.
We deduce from this, by (6.6.b)),

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.15.

Under the hypotheses of (6.13), for every abelian sheaf \(F\) on \(\underline S\), the group of additive \(\underline S\)-functions from \(\operatorname{Parf}(\underline S)\) to \(F\) is canonically identified with \(H^0(S,F)\) (\(S\) = final object of \(\underline S\)), the rank function corresponding to \(F=\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\) and to the unit section of \(\mathbb Z_{\underline S}\).

footnotes.html: we will not use the usual notation \(K(C)\) here, since \(K(C)\) already denotes, when \(C\) is additive, the category of complexes of \(C\) "up to homotopy".

blocks.html: 7. Duality of perfect complexes

7.0. In this section, \((\underline S,A)\) denotes a topos ringed in commutative rings; we denote by \(S\) the final object of \(\underline S\). Our aim is to generalize to perfect complexes the notions of duality well known for locally free sheaves of finite type.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.1.

Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\).

a) If \(F\) has locally bounded cohomology, the same holds for \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\). If \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)\) is pseudo-coherent (resp. locally of finite tor-dimension, resp. perfect), the same holds for \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\).

b) Suppose moreover that \(E\) is of finite tor-dimension (a condition satisfied if \(\underline S\) is of finite type). Then, if \(F\) has bounded cohomology (resp. is of finite tor-dimension), the same holds for \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\). More precisely, let \(a\), \(b\), \(m\), \(n\) be integers such that \(\mathrm{tor.amp}(E)\subset[a,b]\) (or, what amounts to the same, by (5.8), \(\mathrm{parf.amp}(E)\subset[a,b]\)) and \(H^i(F)=0\) for \(i\notin[m,n]\) (resp. \(\mathrm{tor.amp}(F)\subset[m,n]\)). Then we have \(H^i(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F))=0\) for \(i\notin[m-b,n-a]\) (resp. \(\mathrm{tor.amp}(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F))\subset[m-b,n-a]\)).
Proof. Assertion a) is local, so we may assume \(E\) strictly perfect, and we win by dévissage on \(E\) (reducing to \(E=A\)).

For assertion b), we may assume \(E\) strictly perfect such that \(E^i=0\) for \(i otin[a,b]\), and \(F^i=0\) for \(i otin[m,n]\) (resp. \(F^i\) flat for every \(i\) and \(F^i=0\) for \(i otin[m,n]\)). It then suffices to apply the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1.1.

Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline S)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\). If \(E\) is strictly pseudo-coherent (i.e. (2.1) with components direct factors of free Modules of finite type), the canonical arrow of \(D^+(\underline S)\)

\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^{\bullet}(E,F)\longrightarrow\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\]

is an isomorphism.
Proof. The spectral sequence
\[E_2^{pq}=\underline H^p(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^q(E,F)^{\bullet})\Longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^*(E,F)\;,\]
\[\text{où}\qquad\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^q(E,F)^i=\prod_{i_2-i_1=i}\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^q(E^{i_1},F^{i_2})\;,\]
degenerates since \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^q(E,F)^{\bullet}=0\) for every \(q>0\) (1.3.1). We deduce from this isomorphisms
\[E_2^{p0}=\underline H^p(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^{\bullet}(E,F))\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^p(E,F)=\underline H^p(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F))\;,\]
which proves the assertion.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.1.2.

Let \(f:(\underline S',A')\longrightarrow(\underline S,A)\) be a morphism of ringed topoi. Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline S)\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(\underline S)\) be such that we have \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(\underline S)\) and \(\mathbf Lf^*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(\underline S')\). There exists a canonical functorial arrow

\[\mathbf Lf^*\,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\longrightarrow\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathbf Lf^*E,\mathbf Lf^*F)\;.\]

It is an isomorphism if \(E\) is perfect.
Proof. By virtue of the adjunction of the functors \(\mathbf Lf^*\) and \(\mathbf Rf_*\), it suffices to define an arrow
\[\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\longrightarrow\mathbf Rf_*\,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathbf Lf^*E,\mathbf Lf^*F)\;.\]
For this we use the adjunction arrow \(F\longrightarrow\mathbf Rf_*\mathbf Lf^*F\) and the duality isomorphism
\[\mathbf Rf_*\,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathbf Lf^*E,\mathbf Lf^*F)\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,\mathbf Rf_*\mathbf Lf^*F)\;.\]
To see that the arrow of (7.1.2) is an isomorphism for \(E\) perfect, we reduce trivially, by localization and dévissage, to the case where \(E=A\), in which case it is a tautology.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.2.

There exists (cf. SGA 5 I 1.6), for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\), a canonical functorial arrow

\[E\longrightarrow\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F),F)\;.\]

It is an isomorphism if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) and \(F=A\).
Proof. We define the arrow by resolving \(F\) injectively and using the canonical arrow of \(K(\underline S)\)
\[E\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^{\bullet}(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}^{\bullet}(E,F),F)\]
To see that the arrow in question is an isomorphism if \(E\) is perfect and \(F=A\), we reduce, by localization and dévissage, to \(E=A\), in which case it is obvious.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.3.

Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)\). We shall set \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,A)=E^{\vee}\), and we shall say that \(E^{\vee}\) is the dual of \(E\).

By (7.1) and (7.2), if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)\) is perfect (resp. perfect and of finite tor-dimension), so is \(E^{\vee}\), and the canonical arrow \(E\to E^{\vee\vee}\) is an isomorphism. In other words, the functor \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\;,A)\) defines an anti-auto-equivalence of the category \(D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (resp. \(D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) (5.8.4.b))) with itself.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.4

(CARTAN isomorphism) (cf. ([H] II 5.15)). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline S)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\), there exist canonical functorial isomorphisms:

\[\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F,G)\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G))\;,\]
\[\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F,G)\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\mathbf R\operatorname{Hom}(E,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G))\;,\]
\[\operatorname{Hom}(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F,G)\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\operatorname{Hom}(E,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G))\;.\]
Proof: This is standard: resolve \(G\) injectively and \(F\) flatly.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.5.

For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline S)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\), there exists a canonical functorial homomorphism

\[E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\longrightarrow F\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.6

(cf. ([H] II 5.13)). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\). Under one of the following hypotheses

i) \(G\) of finite tor-dimension,

ii) \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(\underline S)\), \(F\) of finite tor-dimension and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(\underline S)\),

there exists a canonical functorial homomorphism

\[\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}G\longrightarrow\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}G)\;.\]

It is an isomorphism when \(E\) is perfect.
Proof. Let us define the arrow. Under hypothesis (i), it suffices to resolve \(F\) injectively and \(G\) flatly. Under hypothesis (ii), it is equivalent, by (7.4), to define an arrow
\[E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}G\longrightarrow F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}G\;.\]
We apply the functor \(\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}G\) to the arrow of (7.5).
To see that the arrow is an isomorphism when \(E\) is perfect, we use the standard method (cf. proof of (7.2)): we reduce, by localization and dévissage, to the case where \(E=A\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.7.

There exists, for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) and \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(\underline S)\), a functorial isomorphism

\[E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\;,\]

with the notation of (7.3).

Proof. Apply (7.6), with \(F\) and \(G\) replaced respectively by \(A\) and \(F\), and take into account the fact (7.1) that \(E^{\vee}\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.8.

Let \(E\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\). There exists a natural arrow

\[\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E)\longrightarrow A\;,\]

which is a "perfect duality", i.e. identifies modulo (7.4) each of the complexes \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\), \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E)\) with the dual of the other (in the sense of (7.3)).

Proof. By (7.5), we have natural pairings \(E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\longrightarrow A\), \(F^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F\to A\), whence, by tensor product, a natural pairing
\[E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\longrightarrow A\;,\]
which, taking (7.7) into account, gives the announced pairing on the \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\). Let us show that this is a perfect duality. We have the canonical isomorphisms

\[\begin{aligned} \mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E,A)&\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^{\vee},E^{\vee})&&(7.4)\\ &\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}F^{\vee\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E^{\vee}&&(7.6)\\ &\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E^{\vee}&&(7.2)\;. \end{aligned}\]
Hence, up to a compatibility verification, we have won. One could also localize and use dévissage to reduce to \(E=F=A\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.9.

Let \(E\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\). Show that there exists a canonical isomorphism

\[E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F^{\vee}\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F)^{\vee}\;.\]
8. Traces and cup-products

8.0. In this section, we generalize to perfect complexes the notion of the trace of an endomorphism of a projective module of finite type (see SGA 5 III for applications). The hypotheses and notations are those of (7.0).
8.1. Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) (5.8.4.b)). There exists a natural arrow
\[\tau\;:\;\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,E)\longrightarrow A\;,\]

composed of the inverse isomorphism of (7.7) \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,E)\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\) and the canonical arrow (7.5) \(E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\longrightarrow A\).

Let \(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\). Applying to \(\tau\) the functor \(H^0(U,.)\), and taking into account the fact that \(H^0(U,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,E))\) is identified with \(\operatorname{Hom}_{D(U)}(E|U,E|U)\), we deduce from this a homomorphism of \(H^0(U,A)\)-modules,
\[\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}\;:\;\operatorname{Hom}_{D(U)}(E|U,E|U)\longrightarrow H^0(U,A)\;,\]
which is called the trace homomorphism(*).
Before going further, let us give some properties of the trace which follow immediately from the definition.

8.1.1. If \(E=A\), \(\mathrm{Tr}_{A|U}:\operatorname{Hom}(A|U,A|U)\longrightarrow H^0(U,A)\) is nothing other than the well-known isomorphism. If \(E\) is a direct factor of a free Module of finite type, a homomorphism from \(E\) to \(E\) over \(U\) is defined locally by
\[e\longmapsto\sum\langle e,x_i^{\vee}\rangle\,x_i\;,\]

where \(x_i\) (resp. \(x_i^{\vee}\)) is a local section of \(E\) (resp.\(E^{\vee}\)); the corresponding trace is \(\sum\langle x_i,x_i^{\vee}\rangle\). One easily deduces from this that if \(E\) and \(F\) are direct factors of free Modules of finite type, and if \(u:(E+F)|U\longrightarrow(E+F)|U\) is defined by a matrix \(\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{pmatrix}\), we have
\[\mathrm{Tr}_{(E+F)|U}(u)=\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}(a)+\mathrm{Tr}_{F|U}(d)\;.\]

8.1.2. Let \(E\) be a strictly perfect complex, \(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(u:E|U\longrightarrow E|U\) a homomorphism of complexes, with components \(u^i:E^i|U\longrightarrow E^i|U\). We then have
\[\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}(u)=\sum(-1)^i\,\mathrm{Tr}_{E^i|U}(u^i)\;.\]

Indeed, the question being local, we may assume that \(u\) is defined by a section of \(\underline H^0(E^{\vee}\otimes E)\), and even that this section is of the type \(\coprod_i\sum_jx_{ij}^{\vee}\otimes x_{ij}\), where \(x_{ij}\) (resp. \(x_{ij}^{\vee}\)) is a section of \(E^i\) (resp. \(E^{i\vee}\)). Making the arrow (7.5) \(E^{\vee}\otimes E\to A\) explicit, we find
\[\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}(u)=\sum(-1)^i\langle x_{ij},x_{ij}^{\vee}\rangle=\sum(-1)^i\,\mathrm{Tr}_{E^i|U}(u^i)\;,\quad\text{cqfd.}\]
8.2. Let \(E\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\). We have seen (7.8) that there exists a canonical homomorphism
\[\gamma\;:\;\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E)\longrightarrow A\;.\]

Let \(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), and let us give ourselves \(u\in H^0(U,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F))\) and \(v\in H^0(U,\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E))\). We can interpret \(u\) (resp. \(v\)) as a homomorphism, in \(D(U)\), from \(A|U\) to \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)|U\) (resp. \(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E)|U\)), and form

\[u\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}v\;:\;A|U\longrightarrow(\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}\mathbf R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,E))|U\;.\]
The map \((u,v)\longmapsto\langle u,v\rangle=\gamma\circ(u\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}v)\) defines a \(H^0(U,A)\)-linear map,
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{D(U)}(E|U,F|U)\otimes_{H^0(U,A)}\operatorname{Hom}_{D(U)}(F|U,E|U)\longrightarrow H^0(U,A)\;,\]
which we shall call the cup-product (*).
The link between trace and cup-product is expressed in the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.3.

In the situation of (8.2), for \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(E|U,F|U)\) and \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(F|U,E|U)\), we have

\[\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}(vu)=\mathrm{Tr}_{F|U}(uv)=\langle u,v\rangle\;.\]

In particular, if \(E=F\), we have

\[\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}(u)=\langle u,\operatorname{Id}\rangle=\langle\operatorname{Id},u\rangle\;.\]

Proof. To simplify, we shall assume \(U=S\). We thus have two arrows \(u:A\longrightarrow E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F\) and \(v:A\longrightarrow F^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\), whence

\[u\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}v\;:\;A\longrightarrow E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\;.\]

On the other hand, we have \(vu:A\longrightarrow E^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\) and \(uv:A\longrightarrow F^{\vee}\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F\). The proposition follows from the commutativity of the following diagrams (whose verification is "purely mechanical"):

\[\xymatrix@C=3em{ A\ar[r]^-{u\otimes v}\ar[dr]_{vu}&E^{\vee}\otimes F\otimes F^{\vee}\otimes E\ar[d]^{\operatorname{Id}\otimes\tau_F}\\ &E^{\vee}\otimes E }\qquad\xymatrix@C=3em{ A\ar[r]^-{u\otimes v}\ar[dr]_{uv}&E^{\vee}\otimes F\otimes F^{\vee}\otimes E\ar[d]^{\tau_E\otimes\operatorname{Id}}\\ &F^{\vee}\otimes F }\]

\[\xymatrix@C=4em{ E^{\vee}\otimes F\otimes F^{\vee}\otimes E\ar[r]^-{\operatorname{Id}\otimes\tau_E}\ar[d]_{\operatorname{Id}\otimes\tau_F}&F^{\vee}\otimes F\ar[d]^{\tau_F}\\ E^{\vee}\otimes E\ar[r]_{\tau_E}&A }\;.\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.4.

\(E\), \(F\), \(U\) being given as in (8.2), let \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(E|U,E|U)\), \(s:E|U\longrightarrow F|U\) be an isomorphism, whence \(sus^{-1}\in\operatorname{Hom}(F|U,F|U)\). We then have

\[\mathrm{Tr}_{F|U}(sus^{-1})=\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}(u)\;.\]
Proof. This is immediate.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 8.5.

If \(\underline C\) is a fibered \(\underline S\)-category, denote by \(E(\underline C)\) the fibered \(\underline S\)-category whose fiber at \(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\) is the category having as objects the pairs \((L,u)\) where \(L\in\operatorname{ob}\underline C_U\) and \(u\in\operatorname{End}_{\underline S}(L)\), and as arrows from \((L,u)\) to \((M,v)\) the arrows \(f:L\longrightarrow M\) such that \(fu=vf\). By (8.4), we can regard the trace homomorphism as a \(\underline S\)-functor from \(E(D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}})^{\mathrm{is}}\) to \(\underline A\) (where the exponent is means that the only arrows considered are the isomorphisms, and \(\underline A\) is the \(\underline S\)-category with discrete fibers defined by \(A\) (cf.(6.6.a))). Since every perfect complex is locally of finite tor-dimension, the forgetful \(\underline S\)-functor

\[E(D(\underline S)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}})^{\mathrm{is}}\longrightarrow E(D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})^{\mathrm{is}}\]

induces an isomorphism on the associated stacks. Consequently, the trace homomorphism extends uniquely to a \(\underline S\)-functor from \(E(D(\underline S)_{\mathrm{parf}})^{\mathrm{is}}\) to \(\underline A\). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Parf}(U)\) (\(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\)), we still denote by

\[\mathrm{Tr}_E\;:\;\operatorname{Hom}(E,E)\longrightarrow H^0(U,A)\]

the corresponding homomorphism, which is a homomorphism of \(H^0(U,A)\)-modules. One sees likewise that the definition of the cup-product (8.2), as well as the assertions (8.3) and (8.4), generalize to the case of perfect complexes (not necessarily of finite tor-dimension).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.6.

Let \(U\in\operatorname{ob}\underline S\), \(E\), \(F\in\operatorname{Parf}(U)\), \(u\in\operatorname{Hom}(E,E)\), \(v\in\operatorname{Hom}(F,F)\), whence \(u\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}v\in\operatorname{Hom}(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F,E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F)\). We have

\[\mathrm{Tr}_{E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F}(u\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}v)=\mathrm{Tr}_E(u)\,\mathrm{Tr}_F(v)\;.\]

Proof. The question being local, we may assume, thanks to (8.4) and (4.10), that \(u\) and \(v\) are arrows of \(K^b(\underline C_0)\). Using (8.1.2), we reduce to the case where \(u\) and \(v\) are arrows of \(\underline C_0\). We finish thanks to (8.1.1).

footnotes.html: The hypothesis of finite tor-dimension on \(E\) is in fact superfluous for the definition of \(\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}\), see (8.5).
same remark as for the definition of \(\mathrm{Tr}_{E|U}\)
